EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado Low moved through the Central Plains and Mid-Mississippi River Valley on March 19. While heavy snow and blizzard conditions were farther south and east, isolated high wind occurred along the Buffalo Ridge across southwest Minnesota in response to a very tight surface pressure gradient. Sustained winds ranged from 40 to 45 mph during the morning and early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds occasionally reached 40 to 44 mph during the morning at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Rushmore. The peak wind gust was 54 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure, clearing skies, and fresh snow cover allowed air temperatures to drop well below zero during the overnight and early morning hours of February 13. When combined with light winds, dangerous wind chills developed, ranging from 35 to 42 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 41 below zero with 41 below zero observed at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Hendricks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures continued to fall heading into Sunday, changing light rain to sleet, freezing rain, and eventually snow across much of western and central Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. ||Total ice accumulations were generally around 0.1 to 0.2 inches for the areas most impacted. Snow accumulations up to 4 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.||Some of the ASOS ice reports were:|0.30 inches at KAXN (Douglas Co).|0.26 inches at KSTC (Stearns Co).|0.10 inches at KRWF (Redwood Co).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations around 0.1 to 0.2 inches of flat ice across the county. Additional snow accumulations around up to 3 inches fell on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, March 4 to Wednesday, March 5, 2025 a powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and strong wind across the Upper Midwest. As is typical with these large spring systems, Tuesday started warmer with highs in the mid 40s. Clouds and precipitation increased throughout the day as the storm organized. In the evening, the rain changed to snow as cold air wrapped around the system. Northwest winds also strengthened, with widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph causing significant blowing and drifting.||A narrow band of 8 to 10 inches, with a few reports near one foot fell from south central Minnesota through northwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow and wind made many roads impassible, leading to hundreds of school closures across the region. There were also power outages, several hundred delayed flights at Minneapolis - St Paul International Airport (and a handful of cancelled flights), and Minnesota National Guard was deployed to help with search and rescue with all of the traffic accidents in southern Minnesota.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|13.0 inches in Dennison, MN (Goodhue).|12.0 inches in Lakeville, MN (Dakota).|12.0 inches in Credit River, MN (Scott).|11.9 inches in Nerstrand, MN (Rice).|11.8 inches in Woodbury, MN (Washington).|11.5 inches in Elko New Market, MN (Scott).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 3 to 8 inches of snowfall was measured across the county with the greatest amounts across the southeastern portion and a tight gradient across Coon Rapids up through Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historically strong low pressure system brought a round of low-end severe storms with damaging wind Friday evening. Widespread light rain spread across much of Minnesota and western Wisconsin Friday night. Rain transitioned to light freezing rain over southwest Minnesota early Saturday morning, resulting in a light accumulation of ice for some areas. By mid-morning Saturday, all of the rain had transitioned to a narrow band of heavy snow covering much of western Minnesota. Snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour and gusty winds over 35 mph resulted in widespread blizzard conditions for several hours.||While it was difficult to get accurate snowfall reports due to the strong winds, some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|5.4 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|4.7 inches in Kensington, MN (Douglas).|2.0 inches in Chokio, MN (Stevens).|1.8 inches in Morris, MN (Stevens).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the western portion of the county. Gusty winds over 30 mph combined with heavy snowfall rates resulted in brief whiteout conditions throughout the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved through the Northland on the 11th into the 12th bringing snowfall to much of the region. The track of the system shifted a bit more to the south than expected and brought higher snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches from the Brainerd Lakes area eastward into the I-35 corridor with some areas seeing almost 8 inches by the time snow ended during the evening of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 7 inches were reported with this event. A high report of 7.8 inches was reported in Alborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties at the start of March. Some improvement was noted across Crow Wing and southern Cass counties with the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was generally near normal to slightly above normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: March began with D2 Severe Drought conditions across a small area along the Itasca County border. No changes in the area of D2 Severe Drought were observed over the month. Precipitation for March was near normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties at the start of March. Some improvement was noted across Crow Wing and southern Cass counties with the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was generally near normal to slightly above normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across much of northern Itasca County through the month of March. Only the far northeastern part of the county was not included in the area of D2 Severe Drought. No changes in the drought categories were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for March was near normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties at the start of March. Some improvement was noted across Crow Wing and southern Cass counties with the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was generally near normal to slightly above normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across the northwestern two-thirds of northern Cass County through the month of March. A very slight reduction in the area of D2 Severe Drought in the southwestern corner of the northern part of the county was observed with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was generally near normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties at the start of March. Some improvement was noted across Crow Wing and southern Cass counties with the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was generally near normal to slightly above normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across much of the northwestern half of the southern part of the county as March began. D1 Moderate Drought conditions were in place across the southeastern half of the southern part of the county. All of the D2 Severe Drought conditions were removed with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was near to slightly above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties at the start of March. Some improvement was noted across Crow Wing and southern Cass counties with the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 18th. Precipitation for March was generally near normal to slightly above normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very small area of D2 Severe Drought persisted in the beginning of March in the far northwestern corner of the county. D1 Moderate Drought covered most of the county with a small area of D0 Abnormally Dry conditions in the far southern reaches of the county. Precipitation and snow melt during the first part of the month led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought in the county, leaving D1 Moderate Drought and D0 Abnormally Dry conditions. An area of low pressure moved across southern Minnesota the 28th into the 29th and brought rain and a few thunderstorms to the Northland. A few of the storms produced hail to the size of quarters during the afternoon. Additionally, a pocket of cold air along the North Shore led to freezing rain across mainly parts of Lake County during the late afternoon and evening of the 28th where up to 0.33 inches of ice accumulation was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was observed along the higher elevation of southern Lake County during the afternoon and evening. A location about 20 miles north of Two Harbors measured 0.33 inches of ice accumulation during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance from Canada moved southeast across the Dakotas into Minnesota February 10-11, resulting in periods of light snowfall. The highest snow totals, exceeding 6 inches in some places, fell along a narrow west to east band just south of Interstate 90 across southeast South Dakota, coincident with the strongest low level frontogenesis. An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.0 inches at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado Low moved through the Central Plains and Mid-Mississippi River Valley on March 19. While heavy snow and blizzard conditions were farther south and east, isolated high wind occurred along the Buffalo Ridge across southwest Minnesota in response to a very tight surface pressure gradient. Sustained winds ranged from 40 to 45 mph during the morning and early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds occasionally reached 40 to 45 mph during the morning and early afternoon at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Florence. The peak wind gust was 56 mph.

The highest snow totals, exceeding 6 inches in some places, fell along a narrow west to east band just south of Interstate 90 across southeast South Dakota, coincident with the strongest low level frontogenesis. An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 25 to 34 below zero during the early morning hours. A Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Lake Benton briefly dipped as low as 35 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three large wildfires began in mid-May and were not contained until mid-June. The smaller of the three fires was the Munger Shaw fire that reach a size of 1,259 acres and was finally contained on the afternoon of the 13th. The larger two fires were operated as the Brimson Complex. The Jenkins Creek fire reached full containment on June 21st with a maximum size of 16,490 acres. The Camp House fire was fully contained the morning of June 13th, reached a maximum size of 12,071 acres and affected around 150 structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jenkins Creek Fire ignited on Monday, May 12, 2025, around 1PM local time. It was detected east of County Road 110 and north of County Road 16 near Jenkins Creek, approximately 15 miles southeast of Hoyt Lakes, Minnesota. It was believed to have been started by a discarded cigarette from a passing vehicle. The fire rapidly expanded due to hot temperatures, low relative humidity, and strong south winds, burning through mixed boreal forest and grassy ground cover, including areas heavily infested by spruce budworm, which contributed to the fire's intensity. The fire initially exhibited extreme behavior, including crown fires and rapid spread through dense forested areas. The presence of dead and dying trees, particularly those affected by spruce budworm infestations, provided ample fuel for the fire's growth. After a couple days, the spread slowed as crews responded, winds subsided, and moisture moved into the area. Firefighters were able to take advantage of that opportunity and built a line that held through another wind event a few days later. Since then, containment continued to be effectively established with full containment achieved on June 21st. The fire reached a maximum size of 16,490 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass and elevated winds resulted in dangerous wind chills across southwest Minnesota during the early morning of January 19. After a brief reprieve during the day, a secondary push of cold air resulted in a return of dangerous wind chills January 20 and then continuing into the morning of January 21. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least one location dipping as low as 47 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 41 below zero with 41 below zero observed at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Trosky.

An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three large wildfires began in mid-May and were not contained until mid-June. The smaller of the three fires was the Munger Shaw fire that reach a size of 1,259 acres and was finally contained on the afternoon of the 13th. The larger two fires were operated as the Brimson Complex. The Jenkins Creek fire reached full containment on June 21st with a maximum size of 16,490 acres. The Camp House fire was fully contained the morning of June 13th, reached a maximum size of 12,071 acres and affected around 150 structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Camp House fire began on Sunday, May 11, 2025, at approximately 2PM local time in Ault Township, about two miles northeast of Brimson, Minnesota, outside the Superior National Forest. The fire was initially reported at 750 acres and expanded rapidly due to dry conditions, gusty winds, and a large volume of dead timber, particularly in areas infested with spruce budworms. It is believed to have been started by an unattended campfire. The fire moved into the Superior National Forest, causing closures and evacuations. Initial fire behavior included crown fires and rapid spread through dense forest, fueled by widespread dead and dying trees. At least 144 structures, primarily homes and cabins along Highway 44, were destroyed by the fire's initial spread. Initially, mandatory evacuations were put in place for Highway 44 and nearby communities, and evacuation centers were established to assist displaced residents. On Wednesday, May 28, 2025, the the local Minnesota Department of Natural Resources (DNR) area forestry assumed management of the Camp House fire. Firefighting operations continued into June with the fire not being 100% contained until the morning of June 13th. The fire reached a maximum size of 12,071 acres and affected around 150 structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance from Canada moved southeast across the Dakotas into Minnesota February 10-11, resulting in periods of light snowfall. The highest snow totals, exceeding 6 inches in some places, fell along a narrow west to east band just south of Interstate 90 across southeast South Dakota, coincident with the strongest low level frontogenesis. An arctic air mass followed the snowfall, resulting in many locations reporting wind chill values of 25 below or colder the morning of February 11.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 25 to 34 below zero during the early morning hours. The AWOS at the Marshall Municipal Airport briefly dipped as low as 35 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three large wildfires began in mid-May and were not contained until mid-June. The smaller of the three fires was the Munger Shaw fire that reach a size of 1,259 acres and was finally contained on the afternoon of the 13th. The larger two fires were operated as the Brimson Complex. The Jenkins Creek fire reached full containment on June 21st with a maximum size of 16,490 acres. The Camp House fire was fully contained the morning of June 13th, reached a maximum size of 12,071 acres and affected around 150 structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Munger Shaw wildfire, located approximately ten miles southeast of Cotton, Minnesota, was detected late in the afternoon on Monday, May 12. Minnesota DNR, along with local fire departments, responded. Multiple aircraft were brought in to help slow down the active flame fronts, supporting the ground firefighting efforts. The fire was active in pine and lowland fuels, but favorable weather conditions led to slow growth of the fire. The fire was believed to have started with the spontaneous combustion of a hay bale. The fire was 95% contained by Monday, May 19th, but was not fully contained until June 13th. At one point during the early days of the fire, the mainline of the Canadian National Railroad was shut down as the fire spread to both sides of the tracks. The maximum size of the fire was 1,259 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures continued to fall heading into Sunday, changing light rain to sleet, freezing rain, and eventually snow across much of western and central Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. ||Total ice accumulations were generally around 0.1 to 0.2 inches for the areas most impacted. Snow accumulations up to 4 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.||Some of the ASOS ice reports were:|0.30 inches at KAXN (Douglas Co).|0.26 inches at KSTC (Stearns Co).|0.10 inches at KRWF (Redwood Co).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations around 0.1 to 0.2 inches of flat ice across the county. Additional snow accumulations around up to 2 inches fell on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, March 4 to Wednesday, March 5, 2025 a powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and strong wind across the Upper Midwest. As is typical with these large spring systems, Tuesday started warmer with highs in the mid 40s. Clouds and precipitation increased throughout the day as the storm organized. In the evening, the rain changed to snow as cold air wrapped around the system. Northwest winds also strengthened, with widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph causing significant blowing and drifting.||A narrow band of 8 to 10 inches, with a few reports near one foot fell from south central Minnesota through northwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow and wind made many roads impassible, leading to hundreds of school closures across the region. There were also power outages, several hundred delayed flights at Minneapolis - St Paul International Airport (and a handful of cancelled flights), and Minnesota National Guard was deployed to help with search and rescue with all of the traffic accidents in southern Minnesota.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|13.0 inches in Dennison, MN (Goodhue).|12.0 inches in Lakeville, MN (Dakota).|12.0 inches in Credit River, MN (Scott).|11.9 inches in Nerstrand, MN (Rice).|11.8 inches in Woodbury, MN (Washington).|11.5 inches in Elko New Market, MN (Scott).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 4 to 7 inches of snowfall was measured across the county with the greatest amounts across the southeastern portion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm brought heavy snow and gusty winds Wednesday morning through Wednesday evening. The worst conditions were confined to the I-90 corridor and southeast Minnesota, with snowfall rates approaching 1 to 2 inches per hour at times and frequent gusts over 35 mph.||Very dry air on the northwest edge of the system resulted in a razor sharp gradient in snow totals, with some counties experiencing both white out conditions and no snow over a small area.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|9.5 inches in Bricelyn, MN (Faribault).|8.0 inches in Albert Lea, MN (Freeborn).|8.0 inches in Blue Earth, MN (Faribault).|6.0 inches in Wells, MN (Faribault).|4.4 inches in Ellendale, MN (Steele).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across the county. Frequent gusts over 35 mph and heavy snowfall rates resulted in several hours of 3/4 mile visibility or less. These conditions resulted in hazardous travel with public works employees reporting whiteout conditions across the county, especially near the Iowa border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved through the Northland on the 11th into the 12th bringing snowfall to much of the region. The track of the system shifted a bit more to the south than expected and brought higher snowfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches from the Brainerd Lakes area eastward into the I-35 corridor with some areas seeing almost 8 inches by the time snow ended during the evening of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were in the 4 to 6 inch range for the event. A high report of 6.5 inches was recorded in Brainerd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the morning of the 17th an arctic front pushed across the area creating white out conditions with blowing snow as winds gusted to 65 MPH in the northern valley. This storm brought Blizzard conditions to Kittson county where there was enough snowpack to blow around with winds measured as high as 59mph in Donaldson. To the south less snow kept conditions from causing a widespread blizzard but winds still gusted over 58 mph in potions of Polk and Marshall counties, and also over in Lake of the Woods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuous blizzard conditions from 9:20 am through 1:40 pm reported and viewed via webcams on MNDOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties during January. No changes in D2 Severe Drought were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for January was up to three-quarters of an inch below normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: January began with D2 Severe Drought conditions across a small area along the Itasca County border. No changes in the area of D2 Severe Drought were observed over the  month. Precipitation for January was up to a half inch below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather occurred across the Upper Mississippi River Valley with several mornings of wind chills of 25 degrees below zero or colder from January 19th to the 22nd. The coldest period with this arctic airmass was the evening on the 20th through the morning hours on the 21st where widespread wind chills of 35 degrees below zero were observed across southeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills began on the evening of the 20th and continued through noon on the 21st in Olmsted County where values dropped between 30 to 50 degrees below zero. The coldest observed wind chill in the county of 44 degrees below zero was recorded on U.S. Highway 63 near Stewartville, MN.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills began on the evening of the 20th and continued through noon on the 21st in Wabasha County where values dropped between 30 to 40 degrees below zero. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties during January. No changes in D2 Severe Drought were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for January was up to three-quarters of an inch below normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across much of the northwestern half of the southern part of the county through the month of January. D1 Moderate Drought conditions were in place across the southeastern half of the southern part of the county. No changes in the drought categories were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for January was up to a half inch below normal.

No changes in drought status were observed over the course of the month. Precipitation for January was up to three-quarters of an inch below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked southeast from southern Alberta through North Dakota and continued into central Minnesota. This was a standard Clipper-type low with limited moisture. The primary accumulating snow started during the evening hours on January 11th, with most of the snow falling overnight into Sunday morning. The dry slot extended up to I-94 and led to periods of freezing drizzle along & south of I-94. The end result was a swath of 3-5 inches of snow across central Minnesota and 1-3 inches along I-94 into western Wisconsin. A thin glaze of ice was also reported in portions of the Twin Cities metro area.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|6.5 inches just south of Pillager, MN (Morrison).|4.5 inches in Rush City, MN (Chisago).|4.0 inches in Mora, MN (Kanabec).|4.0 inches in Little Falls, MN (Morrison).|3.9 inches in Isle, MN (Mille Lacs).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of 1.5 to 3 inches fell across the county. 3.0 inches of snow was measured 4.0 miles NE of Alexandria, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Snow accumulations up to 4 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.||Some of the ASOS ice reports were:|0.30 inches at KAXN (Douglas Co).|0.26 inches at KSTC (Stearns Co).|0.10 inches at KRWF (Redwood Co).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations up to 0.1 inches of flat ice across the county. A narrow band of heavy snow resulted in accumulations up to 2 inches on top of the ice, leading to hazardous travel conditions Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts brought a frigid Canadian air mass and breezy northwest winds during January 17-21. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration extreme cold every took place across the region starting the 18th and lasting into the morning of the 21st. An area of very cold high pressure worked in the Plains on the 18th and 19th and eventually sunk to the south into the Southern Plains by the morning of the 21st. The Northland didn't see the coldest air temperatures of the event being more on the periphery of the high, but did see extremely cold wind chills as low as -54F as the pressure gradient overhead prevented winds from dying off overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -40F were reported in the county.

The primary accumulating snow started during the evening hours on January 11th, with most of the snow falling overnight into Sunday morning. The dry slot extended up to I-94 and led to periods of freezing drizzle along & south of I-94. The end result was a swath of 3-5 inches of snow across central Minnesota and 1-3 inches along I-94 into western Wisconsin. A thin glaze of ice was also reported in portions of the Twin Cities metro area.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|6.5 inches just south of Pillager, MN (Morrison).|4.5 inches in Rush City, MN (Chisago).|4.0 inches in Mora, MN (Kanabec).|4.0 inches in Little Falls, MN (Morrison).|3.9 inches in Isle, MN (Mille Lacs).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of 1.5 to 3.5 inches fell across the county. 3.2 inches of snow was measured near Braham, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts brought a frigid Canadian air mass and breezy northwest winds during January 17-21. This resulted in the coldest air for the region in nearly 4 years with temperatures from January 19-21 into the -10s to -30s F, and wind chill temperatures between -30 and -50 F.||The coldest wind chill temperatures favored west-central Minnesota. Long Prairie Municipal registered the coldest wind chill with -47F at 2355 on January 20, while Morris, Glenwood Municipal, and Alexandria Regional Airports all observed -46F during the afternoon on January 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS located at the Litchfield Municipal Airport recorded an extreme wind chill of -41 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration extreme cold every took place across the region starting the 18th and lasting into the morning of the 21st. An area of very cold high pressure worked in the Plains on the 18th and 19th and eventually sunk to the south into the Southern Plains by the morning of the 21st. The Northland didn't see the coldest air temperatures of the event being more on the periphery of the high, but did see extremely cold wind chills as low as -54F as the pressure gradient overhead prevented winds from dying off overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills as low as -47F were reported in the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of Arctic high pressure made a slow progression across the Northern and Central Plains starting on January 17th and finally departing late on January 21st. Wind chill values on the morning of January 19th generally ranged between 30 to 40 degrees below zero, with a wind chill of 39 degrees below zero observed at the Minnesota Department of Transportation weather station near Graceville, while the ASOS at Wheaton recorded a wind chill of 37 degrees below zero. There was little improvement on January 20th, with wind chills values down to 45 degrees below at Graceville and 43 degrees below at Wheaton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, March 4 to Wednesday, March 5, 2025 a powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and strong wind across the Upper Midwest. As is typical with these large spring systems, Tuesday started warmer with highs in the mid 40s. Clouds and precipitation increased throughout the day as the storm organized. In the evening, the rain changed to snow as cold air wrapped around the system. Northwest winds also strengthened, with widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph causing significant blowing and drifting.||A narrow band of 8 to 10 inches, with a few reports near one foot fell from south central Minnesota through northwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow and wind made many roads impassible, leading to hundreds of school closures across the region. There were also power outages, several hundred delayed flights at Minneapolis - St Paul International Airport (and a handful of cancelled flights), and Minnesota National Guard was deployed to help with search and rescue with all of the traffic accidents in southern Minnesota.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|13.0 inches in Dennison, MN (Goodhue).|12.0 inches in Lakeville, MN (Dakota).|12.0 inches in Credit River, MN (Scott).|11.9 inches in Nerstrand, MN (Rice).|11.8 inches in Woodbury, MN (Washington).|11.5 inches in Elko New Market, MN (Scott).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 5 to 13 inches of snow fell across the county with the greatest amounts and impacts in the western portion. Frequent gusts over 30 mph and heavy snowfall rates resulted in several hours of 3/4 mile visibility or less. These conditions resulted in many county roads becoming impassable or travel not advised early Wednesday morning. MNDOT crews noted that some downed trees also complicated the plowing process.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures continued to fall heading into Sunday, changing light rain to sleet, freezing rain, and eventually snow across much of western and central Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. ||Total ice accumulations were generally around 0.1 to 0.2 inches for the areas most impacted. Snow accumulations up to 4 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.||Some of the ASOS ice reports were:|0.30 inches at KAXN (Douglas Co).|0.26 inches at KSTC (Stearns Co).|0.10 inches at KRWF (Redwood Co).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations up to 0.1 inches of flat ice across the county. A narrow band of heavy snow resulted in accumulations up to 2 inches on top of the ice, leading to hazardous travel conditions Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon of the 17th. A few intense areas of snowfall developed ahead of the front and brought snow squalls that quickly reduced visibility and created quickly accumulating snow on roadways per area DOT webcams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. A snow squall moved from just north of Virginia to the county line south of Babbitt. Webcams in the area showed quick reductions in visibility and rapidly accumulating snow on area roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 6 to 11 inches of snow fell across the county with the greatest amounts in the eastern portion. Frequent gusts over 30 mph and heavy snowfall rates resulted in several hours of 3/4 mile visibility or less. These conditions resulted in many county roads becoming impassable or travel not advised (including MN-13) Tuesday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked southeast from southern Alberta through North Dakota and continued into central Minnesota. This was a standard Clipper-type low with limited moisture. The primary accumulating snow started during the evening hours on January 11th, with most of the snow falling overnight into Sunday morning. The dry slot extended up to I-94 and led to periods of freezing drizzle along & south of I-94. The end result was a swath of 3-5 inches of snow across central Minnesota and 1-3 inches along I-94 into western Wisconsin. A thin glaze of ice was also reported in portions of the Twin Cities metro area.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|6.5 inches just south of Pillager, MN (Morrison).|4.5 inches in Rush City, MN (Chisago).|4.0 inches in Mora, MN (Kanabec).|4.0 inches in Little Falls, MN (Morrison).|3.9 inches in Isle, MN (Mille Lacs).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The vast majority of Morrison County had snowfall of 3 to 5 inches per CoCoRaHS and spotter reports, but there was one report of 6.5 inches in Morrison County, in an isolated location along the Morrison/Cass County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather occurred across the Upper Mississippi River Valley with several mornings of wind chills of 25 degrees below zero or colder from January 19th to the 22nd. The coldest period with this arctic airmass was the evening on the 20th through the morning hours on the 21st where widespread wind chills of 35 degrees below zero were observed across southeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold wind chills began on the evening of the 20th and continued through noon on the 21st in Dodge County where values dropped to 35 to 45 degrees below zero. The coldest observed wind chill of 45 degrees below zero in the county was observed west of Byron, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historically strong low pressure system brought a round of low-end severe storms with damaging wind Friday evening. Widespread light rain spread across much of Minnesota and western Wisconsin Friday night. Rain transitioned to light freezing rain over southwest Minnesota early Saturday morning, resulting in a light accumulation of ice for some areas. By mid-morning Saturday, all of the rain had transitioned to a narrow band of heavy snow covering much of western Minnesota. Snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour and gusty winds over 35 mph resulted in widespread blizzard conditions for several hours.||While it was difficult to get accurate snowfall reports due to the strong winds, some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|5.4 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|4.7 inches in Kensington, MN (Douglas).|2.0 inches in Chokio, MN (Stevens).|1.8 inches in Morris, MN (Stevens).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1 to 5 inches of snow fell across the county with the highest amounts in the western portion. Frequent gusts over 30 mph and heavy snowfall rates resulted in several hours of 1/4 mile visibility or less. These conditions resulted in hazardous travel while snow was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across southern Manitoba into northwestern Ontario brought snowfall to the Northland starting around midday on the first and continued into the morning of the second. Snowfall amounts were generally under 6 inches across most of the region with slightly higher amounts to around 8 inches along the North Shore. Pockets of higher snowfall were seen across parts of the region as well resulting in totals slightly over 6 inches in some locations. The Duluth area was the recipient of these higher snowfall amounts with 6 to 8 inches reported across town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally under 6 inches across the northern part of St. Louis County. However, a few locations around Ely received right around 6 inches with a high report of 6.1 inches six miles north-northwest of Ely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy rain developed Saturday afternoon, reaching peak intensity during the evening. Temperatures near freezing north and west of the Twin Cities led to a period of sleet and freezing rain. Temperatures continued to fall heading into Sunday, changing light rain to sleet, freezing rain, and eventually snow across much of western and central Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin. ||Total ice accumulations were generally around 0.1 to 0.2 inches for the areas most impacted. Snow accumulations up to 4 inches on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.||Some of the ASOS ice reports were:|0.30 inches at KAXN (Douglas Co).|0.26 inches at KSTC (Stearns Co).|0.10 inches at KRWF (Redwood Co).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of light freezing rain resulted in accumulations up to 0.15 inch of flat ice across the county. Additional snow accumulations around 2 to 4 inches fell on top of the ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions Saturday into Sunday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historically strong low pressure system brought a round of low-end severe storms with damaging wind Friday evening. Widespread light rain spread across much of Minnesota and western Wisconsin Friday night. Rain transitioned to light freezing rain over southwest Minnesota early Saturday morning, resulting in a light accumulation of ice for some areas. By mid-morning Saturday, all of the rain had transitioned to a narrow band of heavy snow covering much of western Minnesota. Snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour and gusty winds over 35 mph resulted in widespread blizzard conditions for several hours.||While it was difficult to get accurate snowfall reports due to the strong winds, some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|5.4 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|4.7 inches in Kensington, MN (Douglas).|2.0 inches in Chokio, MN (Stevens).|1.8 inches in Morris, MN (Stevens).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the county with the highest amounts in the western portion. Frequent gusts over 45 mph and heavy snowfall rates resulted in several hours of 1/4 mile visibility or less, especially west of Willmar. These conditions resulted in hazardous travel while snow was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense Arctic airmass moved through the region between February 17 and February 19, bringing dangerously cold wind chills. On the morning of the 17th, wind chills were recorded as low as 45 degrees below zero at the Minnesota Department of Transportation (MNDOT) weather stations located 4 miles southeast of Ortonville and 3 miles south-southwest of Graceville. The Automated Weather Observing System (AWOS) at Wheaton also recorded a wind chill of 40 degrees below zero, with a low temperature of 20 degrees below zero that morning. Additionally, the high temperatures observed at the Wheaton AWOS were only 9 degrees below zero on the 17th and 6 degrees below zero on the 18th. The temperature at the MNDOT station in Graceville did not rise above zero until February 19, when it reached a high of 1 degree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, March 4 to Wednesday, March 5, 2025 a powerful winter storm brought heavy snow and strong wind across the Upper Midwest. As is typical with these large spring systems, Tuesday started warmer with highs in the mid 40s. Clouds and precipitation increased throughout the day as the storm organized. In the evening, the rain changed to snow as cold air wrapped around the system. Northwest winds also strengthened, with widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph causing significant blowing and drifting.||A narrow band of 8 to 10 inches, with a few reports near one foot fell from south central Minnesota through northwest Wisconsin. The combination of snow and wind made many roads impassible, leading to hundreds of school closures across the region. There were also power outages, several hundred delayed flights at Minneapolis - St Paul International Airport (and a handful of cancelled flights), and Minnesota National Guard was deployed to help with search and rescue with all of the traffic accidents in southern Minnesota.||Some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|13.0 inches in Dennison, MN (Goodhue).|12.0 inches in Lakeville, MN (Dakota).|12.0 inches in Credit River, MN (Scott).|11.9 inches in Nerstrand, MN (Rice).|11.8 inches in Woodbury, MN (Washington).|11.5 inches in Elko New Market, MN (Scott).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 to 6 inches of snowfall was measured across the county with the greatest amounts across the eastern portion and a tight gradient across Norwood Young America and Waconia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from the Colorado Rockies during the evening of March 6 and across the Central Plains through mid-day on March 7. A band of precipitation developed along strong mid-level frontogenesis to the north of the low track. With temperatures initially above freezing, precipitation fell as rain, but quickly transitioned to snow and accumulated a broad swath of 2 to 4 inches mainly along and south of Interstate 90. Higher totals from 5 to 7 inches fell across south-central South Dakota. Winds were relatively light and the wet snow minimized overall reductions in visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.5 inches 0.2 miles northwest of Hills.

By mid-morning Saturday, all of the rain had transitioned to a narrow band of heavy snow covering much of western Minnesota. Snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour and gusty winds over 35 mph resulted in widespread blizzard conditions for several hours.||While it was difficult to get accurate snowfall reports due to the strong winds, some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|5.4 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|4.7 inches in Kensington, MN (Douglas).|2.0 inches in Chokio, MN (Stevens).|1.8 inches in Morris, MN (Stevens).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1 to 2 inches of snow fell across the county with light ice accumulations. Gusty winds over 25 mph combined with heavy snowfall rates resulted in brief whiteout conditions throughout the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact but strong winter system moved through portions of the Upper Midwest on March 19th bringing heavy snow, strong wind gusts, and even thundersnow. While snow mixed in with sleet and rain at times, eventually some colder air aloft moved into the region and switched precipitation to all snow with some locally heavy rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour. The heaviest totals in southeast Minnesota generally fell in Fillmore and Houston counties where some locations received upwards of 8 to 10 inches. Additionally, the combination of strong winds and snow led to blizzard conditions in portions of southeast Minnesota where wind gusts over 50 mph occurred in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This winter system brought near-blizzard conditions and snow to Dodge County on March 19th. Snowfall totals between 1 to 4 inches across the county with the highest reported total being 3.7 inches in near Hayfield. Additionally, a peak wind gust of 50 mph was observed at a Minnesota Department of Transportation observation site in Byron during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first week of March, a powerful mid-latitude cyclone progressed through the Upper Midwest overnight on the 4th and into the morning and afternoon on the 5th. As this occurred, rain changed over to snow with increasing winds during the early morning hours on March 5th. Widespread wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph with falling snow led to visibilities falling to a quarter mile or less, resulting in blizzard conditions across portions of southeast Minnesota. Numerous vehicle accidents and spinoffs occurred throughout southeast Minnesota, including one on Interstate 90 that resulted in one non-life threatening injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow and strong winds allowed for visibilities to drop to a quarter mile or less resulting in blizzard conditions during the morning hours on March 5th in Dodge County. At the surface observation station in Byron, MN, a 52 mph peak wind gust was measured during this period. Snow totals across the county ranged from 4 to 7 inches with the highest observed total in the county being 6.5 inches 1 mile northeast of Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of May began with overall dry conditions across the county. Green up had not occurred yet following the winter allowing fire weather conditions to increase during the first two weeks of the month. By the 11th and 12th, very warm, dry and breezy southerly winds were in place and three large fires started over those two days, the Camp House fire, the Jenkins Creek fire and the Munger Shaw fire. The fires spread quickly in those conditions and outside resources were called in to fight the fires. The Munger Shaw fire reached 1,259 acres in size and was contained on the afternoon of June 13th. The Camp House and Jenkins Creek fires were in fairly close proximity and were managed as one as the Brimson Complex. Containment for these two fires was not achieved until mid June with the Camp House reaching 12,071 acres in size and Jenkins Creek 16,490 acres. Around 150 structures were affected by the fires with most of them in the Camp House fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Camp House fire began on Sunday, May 11, 2025, at approximately 2PM local time in Ault Township, about two miles northeast of Brimson, Minnesota, outside the Superior National Forest. The fire was initially reported at 750 acres and expanded rapidly due to dry conditions, gusty winds, and a large volume of dead timber, particularly in areas infested with spruce budworms. It is believed to have been started by an unattended campfire. The fire moved into the Superior National Forest, causing closures and evacuations. Initial fire behavior included crown fires and rapid spread through dense forest, fueled by widespread dead and dying trees.||At least 144 structures, primarily homes and cabins along Highway 44, were destroyed by the fire's initial spread. Initially, mandatory evacuations were put in place for Highway 44 and nearby communities, and evacuation centers were established to assist displaced residents. ||On Wednesday, May 28, 2025, the the local Minnesota Department of Natural Resources (DNR) area forestry assumed management of the Camp House fire. Firefighting operations continued into June with the fire not being 100% contained until the morning of June 13th. The fire reached a maximum size of 12,071 acres and affected around 150 structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of May began with overall dry conditions across the county. Green up had not occurred yet following the winter allowing fire weather conditions to increase during the first two weeks of the month. By the 11th and 12th, very warm, dry and breezy southerly winds were in place and three large fires started over those two days, the Camp House fire, the Jenkins Creek fire and the Munger Shaw fire. The fires spread quickly in those conditions and outside resources were called in to fight the fires. The Munger Shaw fire reached 1,259 acres in size and was contained on the afternoon of June 13th. The Camp House and Jenkins Creek fires were in fairly close proximity and were managed as one as the Brimson Complex. Containment for these two fires was not achieved until mid June with the Camp House reaching 12,071 acres in size and Jenkins Creek 16,490 acres. Around 150 structures were affected by the fires with most of them in the Camp House fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Munger Shaw wildfire, located approximately ten miles southeast of Cotton, Minnesota, was detected late in the afternoon on Monday, May 12. Minnesota DNR, along with local fire departments, responded. Multiple aircraft were brought in to help slow down the active flame fronts, supporting the ground firefighting efforts. The fire was active in pine and lowland fuels, but favorable weather conditions led to slow growth of the fire. The fire was believed to have started with the spontaneous combustion of a hay bale. The fire was 95% contained by Monday, May 19th, but was not fully contained until June 13th. At one point during the early days of the fire, the mainline of the Canadian National Railroad was shut down as the fire spread to both sides of the tracks. The maximum size of the fire was 1,259 acres.

The fires spread quickly in those conditions and outside resources were called in to fight the fires. The Munger Shaw fire reached 1,259 acres in size and was contained on the afternoon of June 13th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jenkins Creek Fire ignited on Monday, May 12, 2025, around 1PM local time. It was detected east of County Road 110 and north of County Road 16 near Jenkins Creek, approximately 15 miles southeast of Hoyt Lakes, Minnesota. It was believed to have been started by a discarded cigarette from a passing vehicle. The fire rapidly expanded due to hot temperatures, low relative humidity, and strong south winds, burning through mixed boreal forest and grassy ground cover, including areas heavily infested by spruce budworm, which contributed to the fire's intensity.||The fire initially exhibited extreme behavior, including crown fires and rapid spread through dense forested areas. The presence of dead and dying trees, particularly those affected by spruce budworm infestations, provided ample fuel for the fire's growth. After a couple days, the spread slowed as crews responded, winds subsided, and moisture moved into the area. Firefighters were able to take advantage of that opportunity and built a line that held through another wind event a few days later. Since then, containment continued to be effectively established with full containment achieved on June 21st. The fire reached a maximum size of 16,490 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In advance of a deep mid-level trough pushing eastward out of the Rockies, a weakening line of thunderstorms moved northeast across the Northern Plains in the early morning hours. A large and dwindling stratiform area supported the development of a strong wake low with wind gusts as high as 70 to 75 mph in portions of southeast South Dakota as well as northwest Iowa. Sporadic tree and building damage were reported as well as numerous power outages, which left around 630 customers without power during the early morning hours along the Interstate 29 corridor in South Dakota and almost 1600 without power in parts of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained easterly winds reached 30 to 39 mph with gusts as high as 50 mph near Lake Benton and Ivanhoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over the Dakotas was slowly moving eastward across the Upper-Mississippi River Valley. Isolated to scattered rain showers developed diurnally, which a few cells across western Minnesota had enough instability ( 100 to 300 J/kg of MuCAPE) to produce heavier rain and a few rumbles of thunder. Given this region of Minnesota was within close proximity of the closed low combined with weak instability, funnel clouds could not be ruled out. ||About five miles southeast of Sleepy Eye, MN a few trained spotters noticed one cell in particular produced a funnel cloud that moved northwest for approximately ten minutes. Spotters confirmed the funnel never reached the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trained spotters provided photos of funnel cloud 5 miles SE of Sleepy Eye, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally 1 to 4 inches, the lack of snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained from 30 to 37 mph and gusted as high as 48 mph at times near Hendricks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted along a cold front and a post-frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening of April 28. Steep mid-level lapse rates around 8C/km and 55 to 65 knots of deep-layer shear led to severe storm structures, which evolved toward smaller-scale linear features along strong outflow pools with 60 to 65 mph winds during the event. Post-frontal storms developed in an environment of up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE which favored mainly large hail.

A quick-moving, somewhat moisture starved upper level storm system moved across the Northern Plains February 7-8, producing 2 to 4 inches of snow across portions of far southeast South Dakota into adjacent portions of southwest Minnesota. While winds were not particularly strong, occasional wind gusts from 20 to 30 mph produced some patchy blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches in Minneota and 2.2 miles south-southeast of Astoria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving Arctic air mass settled across the Northern Plains for multiple days in mid-February, resulting in daily minimum air temperatures below zero. These temperatures combined with light winds to result in dangerous wind chills, mainly during the overnight and early morning hours. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least one location dipping as low as 48 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 48 below zero with 48 below zero observed at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Lake Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historically strong low pressure system brought a round of low-end severe storms with damaging wind Friday evening. Widespread light rain spread across much of Minnesota and western Wisconsin Friday night. Rain transitioned to light freezing rain over southwest Minnesota early Saturday morning, resulting in a light accumulation of ice for some areas. By mid-morning Saturday, all of the rain had transitioned to a narrow band of heavy snow covering much of western Minnesota. Snowfall rates greater than an inch per hour and gusty winds over 35 mph resulted in widespread blizzard conditions for several hours.||While it was difficult to get accurate snowfall reports due to the strong winds, some of the highest snowfall amounts were:|5.4 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|4.7 inches in Kensington, MN (Douglas).|2.0 inches in Chokio, MN (Stevens).|1.8 inches in Morris, MN (Stevens).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the county with the highest amounts in the eastern portion. Frequent gusts over 50 mph and heavy snowfall rates resulted in several hours of 1/4 mile visibility or less. These conditions resulted in hazardous travel while snow was falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Large to very large hail was seen with the first line of storms including a 3 inch hail report just south of Grand Rapids. The second line was more of a damaging wind producing system with a recorded gust of 61 mph at the Brainerd airport as the line moved through. Numerous reports of tree damage were received along with some minor flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree branches were down on the spotter's property. An entire tree was also blown over, however the tree was weaker and ready to fall over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat and moisture pooled near and south of a nearly stationary frontal boundary across eastern South Dakota and southern Minnesota.  Daily highs were in the mid 80s to lower 90s with dew point values in the upper 70s to lower 80s, yielding heat index values from 100 to 115 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index values peaked as high as 104 degrees 2 miles east of Hardwick on August 16 and 101 degrees near Luverne on August 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West-northwest synoptic winds gusted between 45 and 55 mph across southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and early evening of April 14 within a post-frontal cold air advection regime. Scattered showers developed, helping to bring even stronger severe gusts to the surface at a few locations. Peak gusts at these locations ranged from 58 to 64 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aided by rain showers, winds briefly gusted above 58 mph, with a peak gust of 64 mph at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Beaver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak wave lifting out of the central Rockies aided in initiation of a forward propagating Mesoscale Convective system from the lee of the Black Hills southward into the Nebraska Panhandle. With abundant MUCAPE up to nearly 4000 J/kg and DCAPE up to 1500 J/kg, but deep-layer shear only around 20-25 knots, storms retained a somewhat outflow dominant character while on an east-northeast trek. Winds were generally from 60 to 80 mph with significant damage to trees and a few structures along the track of the system north of Interstate 90 into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inch diameter tree limbs downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system across western Kansas lifted northeastward into Iowa March 29-30. Given a very moist airmass and ample lift, precipitation rapidly blossomed across South Dakota and southern Minnesota during the afternoon of March 29. Precipitation fell mostly as rain, but transitioned to light freezing rain late in the evening and into the overnight of March 30 as temperatures dropped. Ice accumulations generally ranged from 0.1 to 0.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice totaled 0.12 inches 2.1 miles southeast of Balaton.

The second round of snow on April 2 was more intense, with additional accumulations of 3-8 inches in west-central MN. ||The highest snowfall amounts over the 48 hours were reported across west-central Minnesota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 5 to 10 inches of snow fall across the county, with the bulk of the snow falling on April 2nd across the northwestern portion of the county. The highest reported snowfall total was 10.0 inches 2 miles WSW of Eden Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated non-severe thunderstorms moved across the region on the morning of the 28th. One of the storms produced a bolt of lightning in the Lincoln Park area of Duluth that hit a parked truck. The truck then started on fire sustaining heavy damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A parked pickup truck was hit by lightning and then started on fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a surface low produced a line of thunderstorms across central and southern Minnesota on April 17th. One thunderstorm had developed along the northern periphery of this warm front which converged on remnant outflow from preceding storms. This convergence led to rapid ascent for this storm to briefly produce hail half-dollar size hail before weakening.||As the afternoon progressed, the cold front associated with this surface low continued to make its way eastward into south-central Minnesota. As this occurred thunderstorms began to develop southwestward along the frontal boundary and produced marginally severe wind gusts. A barn suffered minor roof damage 4 miles south of the city of Easton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo provided via social media.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the course of 48 hours, CoCoRaHS observers across the county measured 3 to 7 inches of snowfall. The snow was split into two rounds, with roughly half of the overall total snow falling on the afternoon and evening of April 1 and the other half on the afternoon of April 2. The highest snowfall total was 6.7 inches located 4 miles SSE of Clear Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a surface low produced a line of thunderstorms across central and southern Minnesota on April 17th. One thunderstorm had developed along the northern periphery of this warm front which converged on remnant outflow from preceding storms. This convergence led to rapid ascent for this storm to briefly produce hail half-dollar size hail before weakening.||As the afternoon progressed, the cold front associated with this surface low continued to make its way eastward into south-central Minnesota. As this occurred thunderstorms began to develop southwestward along the frontal boundary and produced marginally severe wind gusts. A barn suffered minor roof damage 4 miles south of the city of Easton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branch snapped on the southern side of the Faribault Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two upper level waves moved across the Central and Northern Plains April 1 and 2 with multiple rounds of rain, snow, or a rain/snow mix. Initial warm advective precipitation moved across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota the morning of April 1, followed by a relative lull until more substantial wrap-around precipitation blossomed April 2 as a surface low pressure system lifted from the near the Nebraska/Kansas border into south-central Minnesota and western Wisconsin. The majority of precipitation along and south of I-90 was rain, but cooler temperatures farther north allowed for some snow accumulation with the highest amounts along the Highway 14 corridor. Two-day snowfall generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches in Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms had developed across a surface cold front that moved east across Minnesota during the afternoon hours of April 28th. Increased southerly moisture transport from a +50kt low-level jet had reached as far north as the Twin Cities metro which helped destabilize the environment. A few supercells were able to develop and produce hail mainly quarter size to ping-pong ball size although the largest hail stone was golf ball size six miles south of Owatonna.  ||This line of storms also produced two tornadoes in Rice and Goodhue Counties which causing heavy damage to several semi-and tanker trucks, collapsing building walls and a few farm outbuildings. Maximum estimated tornadic wind speeds were 110 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of trees sustained damage and an outbuilding lost its roof and wall panels. The maximum estimated wind was 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of central St. Louis, northern Itasca, Cass and Crow Wing counties at the start of April. No changes were observed over most of the month before the complete removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 29th. This left a mix of D0 Abnormally Dry and D1 Moderate Drought across the region with no drought categories across the Minnesota Arrowhead and along the International Broder. Precipitation for April was around 1.5-2.5 inches above normal in areas where D2 Severe Drought were and led to the removal at the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: April began with D2 Severe Drought conditions across a small area along the Itasca County border. No changes in the area of D2 Severe Drought were observed over most of the month prior to the removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 29th. This was a result of precipitation around 1.5 inches above normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April started out with one last snow storm for the Northland as winter made one last gasp. A Colorado low developed on the 1st and lifted northeast the 2nd and 3rd where it was located over northern Lake Superior that morning. Snow began spreading in from the southwest the afternoon of the 1st and continued on the 2nd before tapering off the evening of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. Widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across much of northeastern Minnesota with heavier amounts to 14 to 15 inches along the North Shore. In addition to the heavy, wet snowfall, strong winds along the North Shore led to a 6 hour period of blizzard conditions along the MN Highway 61 corridor along the North Shore on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the county were in the 3 to 6 inch range with a high report of 7 inches west of Northome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers developed ahead of an area of low pressure across central Minnesota in the Brainerd Lakes area during the early morning hours of the 22nd. Activity was mainly showers with limited instability present. One shower did produce some isolated lightning, including a few positive bolts. One of these positive bolts struck a large tree just south of Loon Lake near the town of Pequot Lakes and caused extensive damage to homes along a cul-de-sac.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A positive bolt of lightning struck a large red pine tree on a cul-de-sac on the south side of Loon Lake just southeast of the town of Pequot Lakes. The bolt caused extensive damage to one of the homes on the cul-de-sac including blowing out two well systems, burning the electric lines into the home, damaged a water line along with a water heater and water softener, blew off multiple tongue and groove boards, insulation and soffit on the house and a shed, destroyed internet and phone lines and all equipment connected to it, and had several wires burn and melt, leading to a smoky haze in the home. All of the homes on the cul-de-sac had some degree of damage, ranging from blown outlets to warped soffit pieces to blown out wells.

A few supercells were able to develop and produce hail mainly quarter size to ping-pong ball size although the largest hail stone was golf ball size six miles south of Owatonna.  ||This line of storms also produced two tornadoes in Rice and Goodhue Counties which causing heavy damage to several semi-and tanker trucks, collapsing building walls and a few farm outbuildings. Maximum estimated tornadic wind speeds were 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were two rounds of heavy snow that occurred from the morning of Tuesday, April 1st through the morning of Thursday, April 3rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers across the county measured 1 to 3 inches of snowfall. The highest snowfall total was 3.4 inches located 2.5 miles S of Mora.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April started out with one last snow storm for the Northland as winter made one last gasp. A Colorado low developed on the 1st and lifted northeast the 2nd and 3rd where it was located over northern Lake Superior that morning. Snow began spreading in from the southwest the afternoon of the 1st and continued on the 2nd before tapering off the evening of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. Widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were reported across much of northeastern Minnesota with heavier amounts to 14 to 15 inches along the North Shore. In addition to the heavy, wet snowfall, strong winds along the North Shore led to a 6 hour period of blizzard conditions along the MN Highway 61 corridor along the North Shore on the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the southern part of the county were in the 6 to 7 inch range for the event. A high report of 7.4 inches was received from Coleraine.

An observer 4 miles west of Clinton would record a multi day total of 9.5 inches, while the COOP observers 2 miles east southeast of Artichoke and in Wheaton would record 8 and 7.9 inches respectively.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 7 to 12 inches of snow fall across the county, with the bulk of the snow falling on April 2nd. The highest reported snowfall total was 10.3 inches in Long Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a surface low produced a line of thunderstorms across central and southern Minnesota on April 17th. One thunderstorm had developed along the northern periphery of this warm front which converged on remnant outflow from preceding storms. This convergence led to rapid ascent for this storm to briefly produce hail half-dollar size hail before weakening.||As the afternoon progressed, the cold front associated with this surface low continued to make its way eastward into south-central Minnesota. As this occurred thunderstorms began to develop southwestward along the frontal boundary and produced marginally severe wind gusts. A barn suffered minor roof damage 4 miles south of the city of Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on June 25th, an outbreak of weak tornadoes occurred across southeast Minnesota which resulted in 6 tornadoes in this portion of the state. These tornadoes were weak and short-lived but did produce localized swaths of tree damage. Two tornadoes also caused minor structural damage in both Houston and Wabasha counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down north of Zumbro Falls and ripped the roof off a mobile home residence, also damaging nearby and heavily damaging a barn on 363rd Avenue. It continued for another 1.5 miles northeast across mostly rural areas and causing sporadic tree damage before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on June 25th, an outbreak of weak tornadoes occurred across southeast Minnesota which resulted in 6 tornadoes in this portion of the state. These tornadoes were weak and short-lived but did produce localized swaths of tree damage. Two tornadoes also caused minor structural damage in both Houston and Wabasha counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado crossed into Houston County from Fillmore County and produced EF-1 damage to trees and one outbuilding roof. This tornado was embedded within a larger area of strong downburst winds that resulted in extensive tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms produced several cases of large hail and funnel clouds, as well as two tornadoes in Nicollet county during the afternoon and evening on Monday, June 16th. The largest reported hail (3.25 inches) occurred with a storm the developed over McLeod county and quickly strengthened over Carver county. There was also significant wind damage in the St Cloud area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage reported near the intersection of Bass Lake Rd and Vicksburg Ln in Maple Grove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall throughout the month of June resulted in the Cedar River at Lansing rising into flood stage during the evening hours on June 26th and then reaching a crest in minor flood stage of 18.74 feet later that night. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 116 degrees with a maximum heat index of 116 degrees at a personal weather station near Hardwick, 111 degrees at a personal weather station in Luverne, and 106 degrees at Road Weather Information System sites near Beaver Creek and Magnolia, all on June 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 105 degrees with a maximum heat index of 104 degrees at a Road Weather Information System site near Trosky on June 20 and 103 degrees at a RAWS site at Pipestone on June 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally 1 to 4 inches, the lack of snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.4 inches at Slayton and 1.5 inches at Lake Wilson. Sustained northwest winds reached 35 to 39 mph at times and gusted as high as 49 mph near Dovray, and sustained as high as 40 mph with gusts up to 59 mph near Trosky. Wind and snowfall combined to result in widespread white-out conditions, especially in rural areas. Roads were closed during the height of blizzard conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached as high as 43 mph near Windom and 42 mph near Mountain Lake, with numerous gusts from 50 to 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spread from the Central Plains early on March 14 to central Minnesota by the morning of March 15. After a period of rainfall and thunderstorms in the evening, precipitation switched over the a band of moderate to heavy snow later in the overnight and early morning of March 15. Very strong winds developed on the back side of the system, with sustained winds from 30 to 44 mph and spotty gusts as high as 59 mph. This produced a short period of blizzard conditions in portions of southwest Minnesota, mainly near the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a period of evening rainfall, snow developed and became briefly heavy with accumulation of 2.5 inches at Tracy and 2.0 inches 5 miles south of Cottonwood and 4 miles south-southwest of Milroy. As northwest winds increased to a sustained 40 to 44 mph at Marshall and Tracy and gusted as high as 59 mph at Tracy, the combination of wind and snowfall led to a period of widespread visibility of less than one-half mile, and frequent visibility of one-quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved into the Upper Mississippi River Valley on February 17th, bringing dangerously cold wind chills along with it. The coldest period was during the morning hours on February 18th when wind chills fell as low as 30 to 45 degrees below zero in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum wind chills on the morning of February 18th fell to between 25 to 40 degrees below zero. The coldest observed wind chill in the county was 36 degrees below zero at Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure spread from the Central Plains early on March 14 to central Minnesota by the morning of March 15. After a period of rainfall and thunderstorms in the evening, precipitation switched over the a band of moderate to heavy snow later in the overnight and early morning of March 15. Very strong winds developed on the back side of the system, with sustained winds from 30 to 44 mph and spotty gusts as high as 59 mph. This produced a short period of blizzard conditions in portions of southwest Minnesota, mainly near the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After rain during the evening, a change to a period of moderate snowfall totaled 1.5 inches at Edgerton and 1.0 inch at Pipestone.  Northwest winds become strong and gusty with sustained winds from 30 to 37 mph at times with gusts as high as 51 mph at Trosky.  The combination of wind and snowfall led to a brief period of visibility of one mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced widespread wind damage and isolated hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter during the afternoon and early evening of Monday, June 2nd. Much of the damage came from downed trees or power lines. The highest measured wind gust was recorded by a MNDOT RWIS site along Highway 9 east of Benson at 78 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph snapped off 10 to 16 inch diameter trees and various limbs at a residence north of Morris along Hwy 59.
