EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northeast through the Central Plains and resulted in the rapid intensification of low pressure as it moved from Kansas to northern Illinois. Conditions started on the warmer side with mainly light rain, drizzle, and a few thunderstorms on the morning of March 4, but a quick transition to snow occurred by evening and was accompanied by a drastic increase in winds to 45 to 60 mph. While snowfall ended up generally 1 to 4 inches, the lack of snow cover prior to this system limited the areas for which the strong winds resulted in blizzard conditions. Much of southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and far northeast Nebraska saw closed or impassible roads by the early morning of March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached as high as 43 mph near Beaver Creek with gusts up to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted within a very moist and unstable environment across central South Dakota during the afternoon of July 29 in response to an approaching upper level short-wave from Colorado. These thunderstorms quickly grew upscale into a line, producing 55 to 75 mph wind gusts across southeast South Dakota and extreme northeast Nebraska, resulting in isolated tree damage.  Weakening storms led to only   isolated large hail in southwest Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed across northeast South Dakota into west central Minnesota during the early evening of July 27 within a volatile environment (MUCAPE 5000-6000 J/Kg) ahead of a southward moving surface boundary. These thunderstorms expanded into a mesoscale convective system and dropped south across the region through the remainder of the evening into the early overnight hours on July 28. Most reported wind gusts ranged from 55 to 65 mph with one location reporting a gust to 70 mph. Isolated tree damage was also observed across northwest Iowa.

A thunderstorm produced high winds and wind related damage to trees and crops west of the town of Barry. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured by a roof top anemometer 2 miles north northwest of Barry. Multiple large limbs were observed broken while several smaller trees were snapped just off the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chasers reported that 6 and 10 inch diameter tree branches snapped at the  intersection of MN-28 and Big Stone County  Road 59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Large to very large hail was seen with the first line of storms including a 3 inch hail report just south of Grand Rapids. The second line was more of a damaging wind producing system with a recorded gust of 61 mph at the Brainerd airport as the line moved through. Numerous reports of tree damage were received along with some minor flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An aspen or birch tree was blown over onto a pole barn causing some structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. A powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region, including northwest Minnesota, brought widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High resolution satellite imagery reveals numerous swaths of significant tree damage in portions of southern Clearwater County MN. This includes one noteworthy area of widespread tree destruction near Long Lost Lake, particularly south and east of the lake. Maximum wind estimated from numerous trees snapped at the trunk blown down in a unidirectional and divergent pattern indicative of destructive straight line winds around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three days of high heat and humidity would result in heat index values topping out at 106 degrees in Clinton and at the Ortonville Municipal Airport on June 20th, 118 and 108 degrees on June 21st and 111 and 116 respectively on June 22nd.

During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. A powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region, including northwest Minnesota, brought widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground survey and high resolution satellite confirmed a long swath of widespread destructive straight-line winds estimated between 80 and 90 mph starting across south central Clearwater County north of Highway 200 and south of Highway 2. The swath was 8 miles at its widest and easternmost point in Clearwater County. This surge of straight-line winds continued east into southern Beltrami and northern Hubbard counties where more significant winds and impacts were observed around Bemidji. The 30 mile long swath started in Clearwater County and ended near the southeastern Beltrami county line. There was approximately 60,388 acres of Clearwater County impacted with widespread tree damage within this single swath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low top or mini supercells developed in west central MN during the afternoon producing brief weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo evidence from chasers and reports called in via trained spotters indicate a brief tornado in this area with no damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. A powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region, including northwest Minnesota, brought widespread straight line wind damage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree branches blown down along with home damage. Winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from eastern South Dakota to Lake Superior on the 16th. As the low tracked to the northeast, it lifted a warm front into central Minnesota. To the south of the warm front and to the east of a cold front, a very warm and moist unstable airmass was in place. Morning thunderstorms across the region left cloud cover in place over much of the Northland, but areas of clearing were able to develop in the vicinity of the low across the central part of the state. This heating led to rapid destabilization and the development of low-topped supercells in central Minnesota. Low level winds were strong and somewhat veered leading to a good environment for tornadoes early in a storm's lifecycle. As a broken line of storms moved in to the Brainerd Lakes area, one storm produced a large EF-1 tornado that began near Casino and moved over Gull Lake before dissipating shortly after moving back onto land near Minnesota Highway 371 just north of Brainerd. This tornado was well documented by chasers in the area. The storm cycled and produced another EF-1 tornado that touched down to the west of Merrifield and moved to the east through the northern part of town and into an inaccessible area along the Mississippi River before ended in Cuyuna State Park along the north shore of Huntington Mine Lake. Extensive tree damage was observed with both tornadoes with many being snapped some ten to 20 feet above the ground. Other areas of tree damage were reported as the line of storms progressed to the east, but no additional tornadoes were found. Some minor flash flooding was reported across the Iron Range as well with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were flooded with water up to the rims of an SUV about 5 inches deep.

An area of severe storms pushed into the Brainerd Lakes region just after midnight on the 23rd and pushed to the northeast across the Minnesota Arrowhead while turning into a bow echo. While there were a few hail reports, this system was predominately a wind producer with numerous reports of trees down along the path of the storm. A measured wind gust of 59 mph was recorded near Tamarack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree was down on a house with other trees also down on powerlines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the second of two tornadoes that were observed and produced damage from a severe thunderstorm. The tornado developed along Moody Lane, just east of Crimson Lane, and moved northeast for about 1.5 miles before turning east-southeast near the south end of Mollie Lake. The tornado then tracked east-southeast for about 3.8 miles before turning east-northeast just after crossing Rebel Road. The tornado then continued across the Mississippi River and into Cuyuna Lakes State Park for about 5.6 miles before dissipating along the north shore of Huntington Mine Lake. Damage along the path of this tornado was almost entirely tree damage with hardwood and softwood trees snapped about 10 to 20 feet above the ground (DI 27/28, DOD 4). Extensive tree damage was observed along Sugar Bush Trail North west of Merrifield with the road completely blocked by fallen trees for a time. The maximum width of the tornado was 900 yards near the south end of Mollie Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system across western Kansas lifted northeastward into Iowa March 29-30. Given a very moist airmass and ample lift, precipitation rapidly blossomed across South Dakota and southern Minnesota during the afternoon of March 29. Precipitation fell mostly as rain, but transitioned to light freezing rain late in the evening and into the overnight of March 30 as temperatures dropped. Ice accumulations generally ranged from 0.1 to 0.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated up to 0.25 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks.

The storm cycled and produced another EF-1 tornado that touched down to the west of Merrifield and moved to the east through the northern part of town and into an inaccessible area along the Mississippi River before ended in Cuyuna State Park along the north shore of Huntington Mine Lake. Extensive tree damage was observed with both tornadoes with many being snapped some ten to 20 feet above the ground. Other areas of tree damage were reported as the line of storms progressed to the east, but no additional tornadoes were found. Some minor flash flooding was reported across the Iron Range as well with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of water over the roadway at the intersection of US-169 and Gary Street with the flooding receding as of midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Large to very large hail was seen with the first line of storms including a 3 inch hail report just south of Grand Rapids. The second line was more of a damaging wind producing system with a recorded gust of 61 mph at the Brainerd airport as the line moved through. Numerous reports of tree damage were received along with some minor flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were snapped and blown over. One tree was uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of severe storms pushed into the Brainerd Lakes region just after midnight on the 23rd and pushed to the northeast across the Minnesota Arrowhead while turning into a bow echo. While there were a few hail reports, this system was predominately a wind producer with numerous reports of trees down along the path of the storm. A measured wind gust of 59 mph was recorded near Tamarack.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust to 62 mph was recorded by the Waukenabo MNDOT RWIS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from eastern South Dakota to Lake Superior on the 16th. As the low tracked to the northeast, it lifted a warm front into central Minnesota. To the south of the warm front and to the east of a cold front, a very warm and moist unstable airmass was in place. Morning thunderstorms across the region left cloud cover in place over much of the Northland, but areas of clearing were able to develop in the vicinity of the low across the central part of the state. This heating led to rapid destabilization and the development of low-topped supercells in central Minnesota. Low level winds were strong and somewhat veered leading to a good environment for tornadoes early in a storm's lifecycle. As a broken line of storms moved in to the Brainerd Lakes area, one storm produced a large EF-1 tornado that began near Casino and moved over Gull Lake before dissipating shortly after moving back onto land near Minnesota Highway 371 just north of Brainerd. This tornado was well documented by chasers in the area. The storm cycled and produced another EF-1 tornado that touched down to the west of Merrifield and moved to the east through the northern part of town and into an inaccessible area along the Mississippi River before ended in Cuyuna State Park along the north shore of Huntington Mine Lake. Extensive tree damage was observed with both tornadoes with many being snapped some ten to 20 feet above the ground. Other areas of tree damage were reported as the line of storms progressed to the east, but no additional tornadoes were found. Some minor flash flooding was reported across the Iron Range as well with this activity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. A powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region, including northwest Minnesota, brought widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust at MNDOT mesonet station 2 miles west northwest of Bemidji BMJM5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, June 20th into the early morning hours of June 21st, much of the region was affected by severe weather. A powerful line of severe thunderstorms categorized as a derecho, which tracked across much of the region, including northwest Minnesota, brought widespread straight line wind damage. After the derecho passed through the region, there was a period of prolonged destructive winds that affected portions of central and east central North Dakota, as well as adjacent portions of Minnesota. This was a unique event due to the long duration of significant winds that followed the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ground survey and high resolution satellite confirmed a swath of destructive straight-line winds estimated between 80-120 mph occurred across southern Beltrami County. The swath started in Clearwater County and continued east across southern Beltrami County and northern Hubbard County, and was approximately 30 miles long. The widest part of the swath was approximately 12 miles wide from just north of Bemidji to its southernmost point in northern Hubbard County. There was approximately 143,838 acres of southern Beltrami County impacted with widespread tree damage within this single swath. Highest impacted areas extend from near the US Highway 2 and Division Street West intersection, through downtown Bemidji, and through southern and eastern areas of Lake Bemidji. Extensive and significant tree damage was noted within this swath. While widespread tree damage occurred across this 30 mile long swath, the most significant tree damage was within a mile swath beginning south of Wilton and extending about 4 miles east of Lake Bemidji. Such widespread and significant tree damage made numerous roads inaccessible, hampering recovery efforts and trapping people at locations like Lake Bemidji State Park. Some trees that were downed are reported to be 100 to 200 years old. As a result of extensive tree damage, widespread and long duration power outages occurred for over 50,000 reported accounts, some locations lasting more than 6 days after the event within Beltrami County near the Bemidji area. Fallen trees also resulted in vehicular and structural damage. Widespread structural damage from destructive winds alone also occurred to residential and commercial buildings, government buildings, the Sanford Center Arena, and Bemidji State University. This includes, but is not limited to, completely destroyed outbuildings and garages, roofs damaged or completely destroyed, and numerous instances of broken windows. Reports of damage to aircraft and airport buildings received at the Bemidji International Airport. Media relayed that 90 percent of Bemidji State University buildings suffered some form of damage. NWS storm survey teams did not find definitive evidence of tornadic circulation, rather they found damage indicative of destructive straight line winds.

A vehicle that got caught in it was abandoned because it would require heavy equipment to clear all the mangled trees. Max wind estimated at 100 mph.

Low level winds were strong and somewhat veered leading to a good environment for tornadoes early in a storm's lifecycle. As a broken line of storms moved in to the Brainerd Lakes area, one storm produced a large EF-1 tornado that began near Casino and moved over Gull Lake before dissipating shortly after moving back onto land near Minnesota Highway 371 just north of Brainerd. This tornado was well documented by chasers in the area. The storm cycled and produced another EF-1 tornado that touched down to the west of Merrifield and moved to the east through the northern part of town and into an inaccessible area along the Mississippi River before ended in Cuyuna State Park along the north shore of Huntington Mine Lake. Extensive tree damage was observed with both tornadoes with many being snapped some ten to 20 feet above the ground. Other areas of tree damage were reported as the line of storms progressed to the east, but no additional tornadoes were found. Some minor flash flooding was reported across the Iron Range as well with this activity.

A complex of thunderstorms moved across the Northland the night of the 20th into the 21st. A warm front was draped across the region and led to a first round of storms moving to the northeast. This was quickly followed by an eastward moving line of storms. Large to very large hail was seen with the first line of storms including a 3 inch hail report just south of Grand Rapids. The second line was more of a damaging wind producing system with a recorded gust of 61 mph at the Brainerd airport as the line moved through. Numerous reports of tree damage were received along with some minor flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust of 61 mph was recorded at the KBRD Brainerd Airport ASOS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of showers and thunderstorms moved across northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 2nd. While most of the storms were sub severe, a dying storm near Grand Rapids was able to produce some gusty winds that toppled a few birch trees across US Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few birch trees were downed from southwest to northeast onto the shoulder of US2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: First of two tornadoes from a severe thunderstorm that crossed through Cass and Crow Wing Counties. The tornado began just north of Casino, Minnesota, and moved northeasterly for about 3.75 miles before taking an easterly turn near the end of Home Brook Lane on the south end of Hardy Lake. It then traveled east-southeast for about 7 miles and crossed into Crow Wing County while over Gull Lake. The tornado continued into Crow Wing County for about three-quarters of a mile moving back on shore then dissipating after crossing Sea Gull Road NW at 1643 CDT. Damage along the path was mainly limited to hardwood and softwood trees that were snapped about 10 to 20 feet up (DI 27/28, DOD 4). Near the beginning of the tornado, it moved across a homestead causing roof damage to the attached garage of a home and pushed the entire unanchored garage structure about one foot (DI 1, DOD 4).

An Arctic airmass and elevated winds resulted in dangerous wind chills across southwest Minnesota during the early morning of January 19. After a brief reprieve during the day, a secondary push of cold air resulted in a return of dangerous wind chills January 20 and then continuing into the morning of January 21. The lowest wind chills during these periods ranged from 35 to 45 below zero with at least one location dipping as low as 47 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lowest wind chills ranged from 35 to 40 below zero with 40 below zero observed at a personal weather station near Reading. The Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Rushmore recorded a wind chill of 39 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening on the Independence Day Federal Holiday resulted in damaging wind gusts across northwest and west central Minnesota. Flash flooding also caused widespread impacts to Bemidji, MN as a result of heavy rain and tree damage debris from the June 20-21 derecho blocking streets and storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of significant street flooding in Bemidji. Tree damage debris from the June 20-21 derecho contributed to blocked storm drains and exacerbated street flooding.

Flash flooding also caused widespread impacts to Bemidji, MN as a result of heavy rain and tree damage debris from the June 20-21 derecho blocking streets and storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of widespread flooding in streets and yards around Bemidji State  University just north of downtown. Tree damage debris from the June 20-21 derecho contributed to blocked storm drains and exacerbated street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms including supercells produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes across parts of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nielsville FD reported multiple brief touchdowns near the pilling station lasting no more than 30 seconds and doing no reported damage other that stripping some leaves off crops. Spotters also reported a funnel with intermittent touchdowns near the Nielsville pilling station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms intensified during the early evening as it traveled southeast along the Minnesota River Valley in south-central Minnesota. The storms were initially more pulse-like, but a locally enhanced wind threat developed thanks to the formation of a rear inflow jet.||Areas of reported damage came out of Brown and Watonwan counties, with several trees and branches being snapped or knocked down. The line weakened as it progress into Blue Earth county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell on a power line, taking out power in Godahl.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly progressed south during the afternoon and early evening hours. Scattered instances of severe wind gusts were witnessed in conjunction with storms that developed along the boundary. The most concentrated damage was reported across Meeker, Stearns, and Sherburne counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KAXN recorded a 59 mph wind gust as a thunderstorm passed through Alexandria MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeastern Minnesota the morning of the 23rd ahead of a cold front. Storms began in Cass County where they produced tree damage and a measured gust of 64 mph in Walker. As they moved to the northeast, they lost their strength, but became heavy rain producers which led to some minor flooding in the city of Ely during the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down on Hummingbird Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level wave embedded in the eastern periphery of a developing ridge produced two periods of severe thunderstorms across much of west-central and central MN on Sunday, July 27th. Westerly flow advected in an extremely unstable airmass with favorable amounts of wind shear to support rotating updrafts.  Late morning convection occurred across western MN producing quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts across Lac qui Parle and Chippewa Counties. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed over western MN and grew into multi-cellular clusters becoming more widespread through the course of the afternoon. These storms also produced torrential rainfall over a short period of time, leading to several roadways being flooded or washed out in Stevens Co near Chokio and Alberta. There was also urban flooding along Hwy 36 and residential areas in Roseville in Ramsey Co. Rainfall rates in these areas exceeded 3 to 4 inches per hour.  ||Mainly quarter to half-dollar size hail was reported across western MN.  The largest hail reports were focused near the Twin Cities metro with quarter size to golf ball size reported by trained spotters. The golf ball size hail fell in Plymouth (Hennepin Co) and one mile southeast of Falcon Heights (Ramsey). As for damaging winds, most severe wind reports measured between 60 to 70 mph.  The strongest estimated wind gusts were about 64 knots or 74 mph, which buckled grain bins near Sanburg (Kandiyohi Co) and damaged power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours on July 28th, a line of intense thunderstorms moved into southeast Minnesota and produced severe wind gusts along with widespread wind damage. Most notably a wind gust of 76 mph was observed at Rochester International Airport along with several other wind gusts over 70 mph across the region. Storms eventually moved south and east into Wisconsin and Iowa as they weakened overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station 7 miles southwest of Lake City reported a 68 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed overnight across the Dakotas and moved across northern Minnesota the morning of the 27th. The storms were severe across parts of mainly St. Louis County where areas of tree damage were reported. Another isolated cell developed to the south of this main area of storms across the Brainerd Lakes area and produced quarter-sized hail as it moved into Aitkin County.

Storms eventually moved south and east into Wisconsin and Iowa as they weakened overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous trees down in the city of Fountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed overnight across the Dakotas and moved across northern Minnesota the morning of the 27th. The storms were severe across parts of mainly St. Louis County where areas of tree damage were reported. Another isolated cell developed to the south of this main area of storms across the Brainerd Lakes area and produced quarter-sized hail as it moved into Aitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell at a campground on Stubler Lake landing on a tent and injuring a 7 year old  boy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front across northeastern and central Minnesota during the early afternoon hours of the 11th. While storms were generally below severe limits, one stronger storm moved through the Brainerd Lakes area and caused some damage to a boat dock and dropped some small hail as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5 to 6 foot section of a dock was pulled out of the water. A few 3 foot in diameter tree  branches were down from trees due to strong thunderstorm winds as well. Winds were gusty for about 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front across northeastern and central Minnesota during the early afternoon hours of the 11th. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms moved across northern Minnesota into northeastern Minnesota during the morning hours of the 10th. Lightning from one of the storms hit a home and started a fire in the Duluth Heights neighborhood just south of Arrowhead Road. Damage caused by the fire was unknown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A structure fire was reported following a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm developed over McLeod County around noon and quickly strengthened as it traveled southeast through the afternoon. The storm produced thunderstorm winds that damaged tree branches and a tree in Sibley county before dropping a swath of large hail from roughly St Peter to Madison Lake. The largest hail report was 2 inches in diameter on the north side of Lake Washington in Le Sueur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many 3 to 4 inch branches down, and one tree knocked down west of Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail as they moved from northeast South Dakota into west-central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter was observed with a storm near the town of Johnson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over western MN and progressed eastward during the evening of July 28th, 2025. As this line of storms reached eastern MN, storm intensity increased, producing 60 to 70 mph damaging wind gusts across the Twin Cities metro down to Mankato.||There were numerous reports of trees down and power outages across several areas.  A couple noteworthy gusts were measured by the Scott County Mesonet in Shakopee at 65 mph.  The Owatonna airport AWOS in Steele Co measured 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in both Cokato and Howard Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level wave embedded in the eastern periphery of a developing ridge produced two periods of severe thunderstorms across much of west-central and central MN on Sunday, July 27th. Westerly flow advected in an extremely unstable airmass with favorable amounts of wind shear to support rotating updrafts.  Late morning convection occurred across western MN producing quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts across Lac qui Parle and Chippewa Counties. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed over western MN and grew into multi-cellular clusters becoming more widespread through the course of the afternoon. These storms also produced torrential rainfall over a short period of time, leading to several roadways being flooded or washed out in Stevens Co near Chokio and Alberta. There was also urban flooding along Hwy 36 and residential areas in Roseville in Ramsey Co. Rainfall rates in these areas exceeded 3 to 4 inches per hour.  ||Mainly quarter to half-dollar size hail was reported across western MN.  The largest hail reports were focused near the Twin Cities metro with quarter size to golf ball size reported by trained spotters. The golf ball size hail fell in Plymouth (Hennepin Co) and one mile southeast of Falcon Heights (Ramsey). As for damaging winds, most severe wind reports measured between 60 to 70 mph.  The strongest estimated wind gusts were about 64 knots or 74 mph, which buckled grain bins near Sanburg (Kandiyohi Co) and damaged power poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across northeastern Minnesota the morning of the 4th bringing a very warm and humid airmass to the region. Isolated thunderstorms developed across north-central Minnesota and were wind producing storms with a gust of 78 mph recorded at the International Falls airport along with some isolated tree damage elsewhere across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 78 mph was recorded by the KINL International Falls Airport ASOS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed north of U.S. Highway 14 near the South Dakota-Minnesota border during the evening of July 18. These thunderstorms drifted south and east along a slow-moving surface boundary and began to train/back-build across the Marshall, MN area. Four to eight inches of rain fell within 2 to 3 hours, resulting in significant flash flooding across the city, especially for areas south and east of downtown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 4 to 8 inches in just a couple of hours led to flooding of numerous roads around Marshall.

Severe thunderstorms including supercells produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes across parts of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked NW to SE with a final S push as it occluded. It tracked for about 5.3 miles, with roughly 0.68 miles in Polk County and the remaining track in Norman County. EF0 damage was noted in Polk County, with EF1 damage in Norman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms including supercells produced large hail, damaging winds and tornadoes across parts of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked NW to SE with a final S push as it occluded. It tracked for about 5.3 miles, starting in Polk County, then continuing into Norman County. After crossing into Norman County, this tornado scoured a soybean/corn field before doing damage to some farm outbuildings. This track may be adjusted pending high resolution satellite imagery. UPDATE 7/22: Track adjusted based on Sentinel-2 imagery. EF0 damage was noted in Polk County, with EF1 damage in Norman County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of scattered storms tracked into Douglas County during the evening on July 22nd. One storm produced a downburst of strong winds as its core rapidly collapsed near the Alexandria Regional Airport (KAXN). No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a very isolated wind gust with no reported damage associated with it. The storm collapsed and dumped what was left of its core over KAXN where the 69 mph gust was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and early evening hours on July 2nd, a few pop up thunderstorms produced strong downburst winds in southeast Minnesota which resulted in localized wind damage. A few instances of downed trees along with some structural damage were reported with these storms. Additionally, small hail up to dime sized was observed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime sized hail 5 miles southwest of Rochester. The report was relayed via mPING.

A few instances of downed trees along with some structural damage were reported with these storms. Additionally, small hail up to dime sized was observed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime sized hail 1 mile south of Spring Valley. The report was relayed via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and early evening hours on July 2nd, a few pop up thunderstorms produced strong downburst winds in southeast Minnesota which resulted in localized wind damage. A few instances of downed trees along with some structural damage were reported with these storms. Additionally, small hail up to dime sized was observed as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported large branches downed and a tree uprooted in Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced high winds and wind related damage to trees and crops west of the town of Barry. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured by a roof top anemometer 2 miles north northwest of Barry. Multiple large limbs were observed broken while several smaller trees were snapped just off the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 61 mph wind gust from a rooftop anemometer at the intersection of MN-28 and Big Stone County Road 59. Nearby crop damage was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced high winds and wind related damage to trees and crops west of the town of Barry. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured by a roof top anemometer 2 miles north northwest of Barry. Multiple large limbs were observed broken while several smaller trees were snapped just off the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One and half miles west of Barry, numerous tree limbs up to 18 inches in diameter were downed and several smaller trees were snapped at the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level wave embedded in the eastern periphery of a developing ridge produced two periods of severe thunderstorms across much of west-central and central MN on Sunday, July 27th. Westerly flow advected in an extremely unstable airmass with favorable amounts of wind shear to support rotating updrafts.  Late morning convection occurred across western MN producing quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts across Lac qui Parle and Chippewa Counties. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed over western MN and grew into multi-cellular clusters becoming more widespread through the course of the afternoon. These storms also produced torrential rainfall over a short period of time, leading to several roadways being flooded or washed out in Stevens Co near Chokio and Alberta. There was also urban flooding along Hwy 36 and residential areas in Roseville in Ramsey Co. Rainfall rates in these areas exceeded 3 to 4 inches per hour.  ||Mainly quarter to half-dollar size hail was reported across western MN.  The largest hail reports were focused near the Twin Cities metro with quarter size to golf ball size reported by trained spotters. The golf ball size hail fell in Plymouth (Hennepin Co) and one mile southeast of Falcon Heights (Ramsey). As for damaging winds, most severe wind reports measured between 60 to 70 mph.  The strongest estimated wind gusts were about 64 knots or 74 mph, which buckled grain bins near Sanburg (Kandiyohi Co) and damaged power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of training thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall that quickly accumulated water up to a foot and a half deep over roadways in Chokio.

Multiple individual storm cells formed along the northern edge of a frontal boundary. These storms quickly strengthened and dropped hail up to 1 inch in diameter before congealing into a line and weakening as they slowly progressed eastward.

A frontal boundary slowly progressed south during the afternoon and early evening hours. Scattered instances of severe wind gusts were witnessed in conjunction with storms that developed along the boundary. The most concentrated damage was reported across Meeker, Stearns, and Sherburne counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed near US10 south of Motley and just west of the Morrison/Todd County line. Relayed via county dispatch.

A slow moving line of storms produced heavy rainfall across portions of southern Minnesota, especially over Blue Earth County. Widespread rain persisted for about 1.5 to 2.5 hours with rainfall rates exceeding 2 inches per hour at times. The rain began just before midnight on the 18th and continued into the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of widespread damage to public infrastructure throughout portions of Blue Earth County due to heavy rainfall and flooding. There was damage to several roads, culverts, and trails, as well as severe erosion to bridge R0410.

The most concentrated damage was reported across Meeker, Stearns, and Sherburne counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust knocked a tree down just north of Hwy 5 on the east side of Waconia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of storms moved from central South Dakota into Minnesota during the morning, then more widespread coverage of storms developed and persisted throughout much of the day. The main axis of heavy rainfall was tied to a slow moving boundary that started across central Minnesota in the morning and progressed towards southern Minnesota by the late evening.||While many locations saw heavy rainfall amounts and rainfall rates at times, two main spots reported flash flooding impacts. This included the city of Albany in Stearns County and the Skyline area just south of Mankato in Blue Earth County. ||There was also an isolated funnel cloud report with a storm that moved through Waseca County that did not produce a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported major impacts in the city of Albany due to heavy rain and flash flooding. This was supported by several public reports of urban and county road flooding, including CR 157 northwest of Albany and at the I-94 underpass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over western MN and progressed eastward during the evening of July 28th, 2025. As this line of storms reached eastern MN, storm intensity increased, producing 60 to 70 mph damaging wind gusts across the Twin Cities metro down to Mankato.||There were numerous reports of trees down and power outages across several areas.  A couple noteworthy gusts were measured by the Scott County Mesonet in Shakopee at 65 mph.  The Owatonna airport AWOS in Steele Co measured 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portion of a shed's roof blown off and Fish house blown around along CR 29 north of Mill Lake or six miles east-southeast of Bowerville. The AWOS station approximately seven miles SW of this location recorded a 45-mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level wave embedded in the eastern periphery of a developing ridge produced two periods of severe thunderstorms across much of west-central and central MN on Sunday, July 27th. Westerly flow advected in an extremely unstable airmass with favorable amounts of wind shear to support rotating updrafts.  Late morning convection occurred across western MN producing quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts across Lac qui Parle and Chippewa Counties. ||The second round of thunderstorms developed over western MN and grew into multi-cellular clusters becoming more widespread through the course of the afternoon. These storms also produced torrential rainfall over a short period of time, leading to several roadways being flooded or washed out in Stevens Co near Chokio and Alberta. There was also urban flooding along Hwy 36 and residential areas in Roseville in Ramsey Co. Rainfall rates in these areas exceeded 3 to 4 inches per hour.  ||Mainly quarter to half-dollar size hail was reported across western MN.  The largest hail reports were focused near the Twin Cities metro with quarter size to golf ball size reported by trained spotters. The golf ball size hail fell in Plymouth (Hennepin Co) and one mile southeast of Falcon Heights (Ramsey). As for damaging winds, most severe wind reports measured between 60 to 70 mph.  The strongest estimated wind gusts were about 64 knots or 74 mph, which buckled grain bins near Sanburg (Kandiyohi Co) and damaged power poles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a MNDOT road weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed north of U.S. Highway 14 near the South Dakota-Minnesota border during the evening of July 18. These thunderstorms drifted south and east along a slow-moving surface boundary and began to train/back-build across the Marshall, MN area. Four to eight inches of rain fell within 2 to 3 hours, resulting in significant flash flooding across the city, especially for areas south and east of downtown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marshall Fire Department performed a water rescue for a person trapped in a flooded car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms moved through the Brainerd Lakes area into Aitkin and St. Louis counties during the afternoon hours of the 22nd in association with a warm front moving through the region. A few of the storms produced wind damage to trees in the area and heavy rainfall led to ponding water on an area golf course.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on power lines in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeastern Minnesota the morning of the 23rd ahead of a cold front. Storms began in Cass County where they produced tree damage and a measured gust of 64 mph in Walker. As they moved to the northeast, they lost their strength, but became heavy rain producers which led to some minor flooding in the city of Ely during the late morning hours.

A strong winter system pivoted over Minnesota and Wisconsin on December 28th, allowing widespread snow to last nearly 24 hours. Surface temperatures started near or slightly above freezing on the morning of the 28th, resulting in a few hours of rain before a rapid cool down occurred and rain transitioned to all snow. As this transition happened, some areas experienced a flash freeze and winds increased out of the northwest, gusting to around 35 to 50 mph. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow dropped visibilities as low as 1/4 mile and resulted in very difficult travel, especially across portions of western and southern Minnesota where winds were strongest.||Although the snow ended at night on the 28th, reduced visibilities and travel impacts lingered into the morning of the 29th due to blowing snow. Most areas reported at least 3 to 5 inches of snowfall, with the highest amounts around 6 to 9 inches in south-central Minnesota. Governor Walz deployed the National Guard to assist with rescue operations for stranded motorists in southern Minnesota.||The top snowfall reports were:|9.0 inches in Mankato, MN (Blue Earth).|8.3 inches in Lake Crystal, MN (Blue Earth).|8.0 inches 2 miles WNW of Danube, MN (Renville).|8.0 inches 3 miles WNW of Madison Lake, MN (Blue Earth).|7.5 inches in Burnsville, MN (Dakota).|7.5 inches 6 miles NNW of Le Center, MN (Le Sueur).|7.2 inches 5 miles SE of Forest Lake, MN (Washington).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of falling snow and strong winds resulted in prolonged blizzard conditions throughout the county. Surface visibilities reported at KGDB in Granite Falls dropped to 1/4 mile for several hours, and winds gusted around 35 to 40 mph while snow was falling throughout much of the 28th. The Minnesota Department of Transportation advised against non-essential travel due to very difficult travel conditions and MNDoT road cameras showed near zero visibility throughout the county. Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches throughout the county.

A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29. The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation. As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa. State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th. Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes. Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two episodes of extended blizzard conditions occurred over a slightly less than 24 hour period, with a slight improvement in conditions during the mid-afternoon to early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 45 mph were concurrent with light to moderate snowfall, which totaled 3.0 inches near Arco.  With frequent whiteout conditions reported, travel was not advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter system pivoted over Minnesota and Wisconsin on December 28th, allowing widespread snow to last nearly 24 hours. Surface temperatures started near or slightly above freezing on the morning of the 28th, resulting in a few hours of rain before a rapid cool down occurred and rain transitioned to all snow. As this transition happened, some areas experienced a flash freeze and winds increased out of the northwest, gusting to around 35 to 50 mph. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow dropped visibilities as low as 1/4 mile and resulted in very difficult travel, especially across portions of western and southern Minnesota where winds were strongest.||Although the snow ended at night on the 28th, reduced visibilities and travel impacts lingered into the morning of the 29th due to blowing snow. Most areas reported at least 3 to 5 inches of snowfall, with the highest amounts around 6 to 9 inches in south-central Minnesota. Governor Walz deployed the National Guard to assist with rescue operations for stranded motorists in southern Minnesota.||The top snowfall reports were:|9.0 inches in Mankato, MN (Blue Earth).|8.3 inches in Lake Crystal, MN (Blue Earth).|8.0 inches 2 miles WNW of Danube, MN (Renville).|8.0 inches 3 miles WNW of Madison Lake, MN (Blue Earth).|7.5 inches in Burnsville, MN (Dakota).|7.5 inches 6 miles NNW of Le Center, MN (Le Sueur).|7.2 inches 5 miles SE of Forest Lake, MN (Washington).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy falling snow and strong winds resulted in prolonged blizzard conditions throughout the county. Surface visibilities reported at KBDH in Willmar dropped to 1/4 mile or less for over 12 hours straight, and winds gusted around 35 to 45 mph while snow was falling throughout much of the 28th. The Minnesota Department of Transportation advised against non-essential travel due to very difficult travel conditions. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overnight mesoscale convective system developed over eastern South Dakota and southwest Minnesota early Saturday morning on August 16th. The bowing system grew in scale as it overspread portions of western and central Minnesota. There were several measured wind gusts over 60 mph, and even a couple over 70 mph in eastern Yellow Medicine county. Most of the wind damage reports of downed trees and powerlines were delayed and estimated using radar given that the storms moved through early in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station MN059 T.h.212 - Marietta -  Mp 1 measured a 57 kt wind gust at 3:40am and 3:45am LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system would provide rainfall in the early morning hours that would transition to snow behind an arctic front producing strong wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph. Given the wet road surfaces and rapidly falling temperatures, flash freezing concerns existed for areas across central Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro. Across portions of south-central and southern Minnesota, strong winds caused falling snow to turn into blowing snow leading to blizzard conditions. ||The strongest wind gusts from this event were: |Willmar at 60 mph (Kandiyohi)|Redwood Falls at 55 mph (Redwood)|Montevideo at 51 mph (Chippewa)|Olivia at 49 mph (Renville)|New Ulm at 49 mph (Brown)|Le Sueur at 45 mph (Le Sueur).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow combined with strong 25 to 35 mph wind gusts caused blowing snow and visibility near at 1/4 mile or below at the RWIS site near Medford. Emergency Management reported the blowing snow caused 25 vehicles to spinout, or crash across the county due to the whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight, a slow moving convective cluster within an environment with a strong low-level jet and abundant deep-layer moisture slid from western to southern Minnesota. Storms continually regenerated on the southwest flank of the cluster, with a boundary remaining stationary for several hours from south of Marshall to north of Westbrook. Some areas had more spotty heavy rainfall the prior night, and new rainfall amounts were generally from 2 to 4 inches in the band with radar estimates between of 4 to 6 inches. Numerous city and some rural roadways were inundated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused water to overrun 170th Street between 190th and 200th Avenue. The roadway was closed as water remained ponded over the road for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled boundary was situated across Minnesota with a northwest to southeast orientation. With just enough instability and moisture, scattered storms developed along the boundary during the afternoon and lasted into the early evening. Despite there being limited low-level shear, interactions from storm outflows provided a favorable environment for brief, non-mesocyclonic, funnel clouds to form. ||Out of the several funnel clouds during the day, only one was verified touching down. This was near Kenyon and Wannamingo in Goodhue County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a brief funnel cloud near the intersection of MN-99 and 145th Ave  in eastern Le Sueur county.

A strong upper level wave moved from the northern Rockies into southern Canada, brushing through eastern South Dakota and stirring up a 35-45 knot low-level jet within an environment with around 2000 J/kg of elevated MUCAPE. By evening, a well-organized convective system developed across northeast South Dakota and dropped slowly southeast into southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa through the overnight hours. Along the track, stronger cells produced large hail up to golf ball size and scattered wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several individual rounds of thunderstorms impacted southern Minnesota from Friday, August 16th through Sunday, August 18th. Heavy rain amounts on the order of 5-10 inches fell over the course of 72 hours, most of which fell from late Saturday into early Sunday, leading to several cases of flooded roadways and damaged buildings. A strong upper level wave moved from the northern Rockies into southern Canada, brushing through eastern South Dakota and stirring up a 35-45 knot low-level jet within an environment with around 2000 J/kg of elevated MUCAPE. By evening, a well-organized convective system developed across northeast South Dakota and dropped slowly southeast into southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa through the overnight hours. Along the track, stronger cells produced large hail up to golf ball size and scattered wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree along U.S. Highway 14 near the South Dakota/Minnesota border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Onshore winds led to waves of around 2 to 4 feet with occasional higher waves along the North Shore of Lake Superior during the day on August 12th. A man walking on the beach at a resort in Lutsen was knocked over by the waves and subsequently drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A QLCS type mesoscale convective system brought widespread severe thunderstorm winds to northwest Minnesota during the early morning hours. A tornado associated with this MCS also tracked from Grand Forks, ND to East Grand Forks, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado produced intermittent EF-0 and EF-1 damage in Grand Forks, ND and East Grand Forks, MN. It initially touched down near the intersection of 38th St. and 32nd Ave in Grand Forks, ND where it bent a light pole from the top of the intersection stop lights. It tracked to the northeast just south of Altru Hospital along 17th Ave S., past Lewis and Clark elementary School, and then crossed the Red River of the North at Lincoln Drive Park into Minnesota. It lifted over the southern part of East Grand Forks, MN in western Polk County around the Red Lake River near Folsom Park. Most of the damage was to trees. The maximum rating for the tornado was EF-1 in Grand Forks County ND. The rating in Polk County is EF-0 based on a max wind estimated at 80 mph winds from hardwood tree damage before it dissipated in East Grand Forks, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over the Red River Valley region during the early morning hours of the 8th and pushed into central and northeastern Minnesota during the mid to late morning hours. The storms intensified as they moved into the Brainerd Lakes region with bowing segments producing strong winds. By the late morning hours, the storms outran their support leading to a quick drop in intensity as they reached Lake Superior and northwestern Wisconsin. Numerous reports of tree damage were received from the Brainerd Lakes area into Aitkin and St. Louis counties. A lightning strike at a cemetery damaged headstones as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on Turtle Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over the Red River Valley region during the early morning hours of the 8th and pushed into central and northeastern Minnesota during the mid to late morning hours. The storms intensified as they moved into the Brainerd Lakes region with bowing segments producing strong winds. By the late morning hours, the storms outran their support leading to a quick drop in intensity as they reached Lake Superior and northwestern Wisconsin. Numerous reports of tree damage were received from the Brainerd Lakes area into Aitkin and St. Louis counties. A lightning strike at a cemetery damaged headstones as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were down on a parking lot. A few poplar tree tops were snapped as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter system pivoted over Minnesota and Wisconsin on December 28th, allowing widespread snow to last nearly 24 hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms persisted through much of the day in southern Minnesota, though the strongest cells developed during the late afternoon after destabilization of the atmosphere was able to occur. These storms produced a handful of reported cases of severe hail up to golf ball sized as they traveled southeast and dissipated by around 10pm local time.

A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29. The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation. As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa. State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th. Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes. Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two episodes of extended blizzard conditions occurred over a slightly less than 24 hour period, with a slight improvement in conditions during the late afternoon to early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 50 mph were concurrent with light to moderate snowfall, which totaled 2.0 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield. With frequent whiteout conditions reported, travel was not advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm developed ahead of a decaying area of showers across Aitkin County during the late morning hours of the 15th. Only one damage report was received from the storm which was a few downed trees. One tree was toppled ripping the root ball from the ground with another falling on a garage causing minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four medium to large trees were down with one uprooted with the full root ball. One tree fell on garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper type system moved from the Pacific Northwest across the Northern Plains where it deepened and brought a period of light freezing rain less than one quarter of an inch followed by intense bands of heavy snow. The greatest impacts occurred where slick roads combined with heavy snow rates greater than 1 inch per hour causing near zero visibility across northeast North Dakota and parts of northwest and west central Minnesota. No travel advised was issued by the NDDOT with icy and snow covered roads reported by the MNDOT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on reports from the surrounding counties we can extrapolate that a swath of 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across Red Lake County, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impactful winter storm brought strong winds and snow to portions of southeast Minnesota throughout the afternoon and evening hours on December 28th. Snowfall totals across southeast Minnesota ranged from 2 to 7 inches with the highest amounts being north of Interstate 90. In addition, the combination of wind gusts of 35 to 55 mph resulted in visibilities dropping to a quarter of a mile or less at times resulting in blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of falling snow and wind gusts of around 40 to 55 mph resulted in very low visibilities during the afternoon and evening hours on December 28th in portions of Olmsted County. During this period, a peak wind gust of 52 mph was observed at Rochester International Airport. Totals snow accumulations across the county ranged from 5 to 7 inches with the highest reported snowfall amount being 6.5 inches 3 miles north-northwest of Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper type system moved through the Northern Plains and upper Midwest, and brought hazardous winter impacts and high winds to eastern North Dakota and northwest and west central Minnesota. Initially rain and freezing rain fell ahead of the snow, followed by flash freeze conditions as light to moderate snow developed and brought 1 to 4 inches of snowfall to northwest Minnesota, with a localized region of heavier snow over the Northwest Angle in Lake of the Woods County. Peak winds were in the 60 to 72 mph range during this evening and combined with falling snow to produce blizzard conditions in the Red River Valley of northwest Minnesota. The icy roads at the onset of the event exacerbated difficult to impossible travel as blizzard conditions developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several NDAWN and Mesonet sites reported wind gusts from 60 to 62 mph during the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29. The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation. As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa. State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th. Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes. Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two episodes of extended blizzard conditions occurred over a slightly less than 24 hour period, with a slight improvement in conditions during the mid-afternoon to early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 45 mph were concurrent with light to moderate snowfall, which totaled 1.5 inches at Lake Wilson. With frequent whiteout conditions reported, travel was not advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface cold front moved across the northern plains on December 12th. The front featured a brief period of light snow in the early morning across western Minnesota, then a mixture of light snow and freezing precipitation across eastern Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro around the morning rush-hour. The greatest impacts occurred across western Minnesota where a cold airmass increased winds to 25-35 mph. In areas where snow had just recently fallen, the increased winds created periods of reduced visibility as a result of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS located at the Willmar Municipal Airport reported gusty winds near 35 mph and visibility falling below a half mile due to blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system would provide rainfall in the early morning hours that would transition to snow behind an arctic front producing strong wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow combined with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts created a period of blowing snow across the county. The ASOS station located at Flying Cloud Regional Airport recorded a wind gust of 38 mph and periods where visibility fell between one quarter to one half mile due to blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved northwest to southeast to the south of the Northland on the 9th into the 10th. This track brought an area of heavy snowfall from the Brainerd Lakes and Walker areas eastward into the I-35 corridor and into parts of northwest Wisconsin. Snowfall totals in this area were in the 5 to 7 inch range with a few areas seeing up to around 8 inches of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals for the event were in the 4 to 7 inch range across the northern part of the county. A few reports of 8 inches were received from he Hackensack area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper type system moved from the Pacific Northwest across the Northern Plains where it deepened and brought a period of light freezing rain less than one quarter of an inch followed by intense bands of heavy snow. The greatest impacts occurred where slick roads combined with heavy snow rates greater than 1 inch per hour causing near zero visibility across northeast North Dakota and parts of northwest and west central Minnesota. No travel advised was issued by the NDDOT with icy and snow covered roads reported by the MNDOT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measurement of 6.5 inches of snow 3 N of Menahga in far northwest Wadena County at CoCoRaHS station MN WD-11. Other nearby reports in surrounding counties allow us to extrapolate that 50 percent of Wadena County received 6-6.5 inches of snow including along the US 71 corridor north of Wadena, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short duration but high impact winter system moved through the region on December 18th bringing very strong winds and snow to portions of southeast Minnesota. This resulted in a period of blizzard conditions as visibilities reduced below a quarter of a mile at times in combination with wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph during the afternoon. Numerous vehicle slide offs occurred in addition to two accidents in Mower County that resulted in non-life threatening injuries to two individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of falling snow and high winds led to a period of very low visibility during the afternoon and evening on December 18th. Snow accumulations were generally around an inch or less. Additionally, a peak wind gust of 58 mph was observed at a Rochester International Airport during this period.

A quick-moving low pressure system dropped southeastward across the Northern Plains on December 6. Light snow resulted in accumulations from 1 to 5 inches across southwest Minnesota. Roads were slick and snow covered, resulting in hazardous travel. Some patchy freezing drizzle fell as well after the bulk of the snow moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches at Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper type system moved through the Northern Plains and upper Midwest, and brought hazardous winter impacts and high winds to eastern North Dakota and northwest and west central Minnesota. Initially rain and freezing rain fell ahead of the snow, followed by flash freeze conditions as light to moderate snow developed and brought 1 to 4 inches of snowfall to northwest Minnesota, with a localized region of heavier snow over the Northwest Angle in Lake of the Woods County. Peak winds were in the 60 to 72 mph range during this evening and combined with falling snow to produce blizzard conditions in the Red River Valley of northwest Minnesota. The icy roads at the onset of the event exacerbated difficult to impossible travel as blizzard conditions developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NDAWN station, located at Rothsay 2W, recorded a 10m wind gust of 58mph. Wind gusts up to 60 mph were reported at a Mesonet station (MN014) 1 mile north of Rothsay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter system pivoted over Minnesota and Wisconsin on December 28th, allowing widespread snow to last nearly 24 hours. Surface temperatures started near or slightly above freezing on the morning of the 28th, resulting in a few hours of rain before a rapid cool down occurred and rain transitioned to all snow. As this transition happened, some areas experienced a flash freeze and winds increased out of the northwest, gusting to around 35 to 50 mph. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow dropped visibilities as low as 1/4 mile and resulted in very difficult travel, especially across portions of western and southern Minnesota where winds were strongest.||Although the snow ended at night on the 28th, reduced visibilities and travel impacts lingered into the morning of the 29th due to blowing snow. Most areas reported at least 3 to 5 inches of snowfall, with the highest amounts around 6 to 9 inches in south-central Minnesota. Governor Walz deployed the National Guard to assist with rescue operations for stranded motorists in southern Minnesota.||The top snowfall reports were:|9.0 inches in Mankato, MN (Blue Earth).|8.3 inches in Lake Crystal, MN (Blue Earth).|8.0 inches 2 miles WNW of Danube, MN (Renville).|8.0 inches 3 miles WNW of Madison Lake, MN (Blue Earth).|7.5 inches in Burnsville, MN (Dakota).|7.5 inches 6 miles NNW of Le Center, MN (Le Sueur).|7.2 inches 5 miles SE of Forest Lake, MN (Washington).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of falling snow and breezy winds resulted in winter storm conditions throughout the county. Snowfall reports ranged from 4 to 6, and the highest report was 6.0 inches in Red Wing. Surface visibilities reported at KSYN in Stanton dropped to 1 mile or less for several hours, and winds gusted to around 25 to 30 mph while snow was falling throughout much of the 28th.||There were difficult travel conditions which resulted in a semi-truck rollover near the intersection of Highway 52 and County Rd 11 in Pine Island, MN. The driver was injured and taken to a nearby hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplifying clipper system raced from southern Alberta to Wisconsin from later on December 9 through midday on December 10. Intense synoptic winds developed on the back side of the system behind the surging cold front. Widespread northwest wind gusts reached 50 to 70 mph over several hours and resulted in considerable small limb damage and some spotty infrastructure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Synoptic northwest winds were occasionally sustained from 40 to 50 mph at an Automated Weather Observing System site at Marshall with gusts at high as 65 mph at a Minnesota Road Weather Information System site at Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter system pivoted over Minnesota and Wisconsin on December 28th, allowing widespread snow to last nearly 24 hours. Surface temperatures started near or slightly above freezing on the morning of the 28th, resulting in a few hours of rain before a rapid cool down occurred and rain transitioned to all snow. As this transition happened, some areas experienced a flash freeze and winds increased out of the northwest, gusting to around 35 to 50 mph. The combination of strong winds and heavy snow dropped visibilities as low as 1/4 mile and resulted in very difficult travel, especially across portions of western and southern Minnesota where winds were strongest.||Although the snow ended at night on the 28th, reduced visibilities and travel impacts lingered into the morning of the 29th due to blowing snow. Most areas reported at least 3 to 5 inches of snowfall, with the highest amounts around 6 to 9 inches in south-central Minnesota. Governor Walz deployed the National Guard to assist with rescue operations for stranded motorists in southern Minnesota.||The top snowfall reports were:|9.0 inches in Mankato, MN (Blue Earth).|8.3 inches in Lake Crystal, MN (Blue Earth).|8.0 inches 2 miles WNW of Danube, MN (Renville).|8.0 inches 3 miles WNW of Madison Lake, MN (Blue Earth).|7.5 inches in Burnsville, MN (Dakota).|7.5 inches 6 miles NNW of Le Center, MN (Le Sueur).|7.2 inches 5 miles SE of Forest Lake, MN (Washington).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of falling snow and breezy winds resulted in winter storm conditions throughout the county. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 7 inches, and the highest report was 6.5 inches in Otsego. Surface visibilities reported at KMGG in Maple Lake dropped to 1 mile or less for several hours, and winds gusted to around 30 to 35 mph while snow was falling throughout much of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure moved across North Dakota and northern Minnesota early on December 18th. A very strong cold front plowed southward behind the system and was accompanied by a minimal amount of snowfall over several hours during the later morning and afternoon hours. While most snowfall was limited to a half inch to inch at most, increasing collocation of the snow and strong wind east of Interstate 29 resulted in areas of blizzard or near blizzard conditions across parts of southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. Several injury accidents were reported across northwest Iowa with the Department of Transportation advising no travel for several hours in the afternoon. The high winds also produced some spotty power outages. Numerous schools delayed release to allow for conditions to improve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds occasionally reached 30 to 37 mph with a peak gust of 56 mph 4 miles west-southwest of Mountain Lake, 55 mph 4 miles east-northeast of Jeffers and 48 mph at Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29. The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation. As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa. State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th. Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes. Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent short duration near-blizzard conditions occurred, divided by a longer improvement in conditions as falling snow ended for a time during the late afternoon and evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 50 mph were concurrent with light snowfall, which totaled 0.5 inch at Hills and resulted in brief whiteout conditions that led to no travel advised for a time midday Saturday.

With frequent whiteout conditions reported, travel was not advised.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small band of primarily freezing rain and sleet developed across southwestern Minnesota then tracked east across central and eastern Minnesota. The band of precipitation produced an hour or two of a light wintry mix resulting in minor ice and sleet accumulation before changing into all snow. As a result of the freezing rain, slick travel conditions existed during the morning commute. Travel advisories were issued by MnDOT for portions of south-central MN due to slippery roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observers reported a glaze of ice approximately 8 miles south of Prior Lake and a coating of ice pellets in Savage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a dreary holiday with periods of rain, freezing drizzle, and fog impacting travel conditions throughout the day. The freezing precipitation resulted in light icing, primarily in areas north of the Twin Cities metro and north of I-94 in central Minnesota, with various reports noting anywhere from a glaze to a couple hundredths of ice accumulation. The worst travel conditions from the freezing precipitation were mainly late Christmas evening and night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Rush City reported several hours of freezing drizzle while temperatures were at or below freezing. Multiple CoCoRaHS observers reported light icing on roads and sidewalks.

Several injury accidents were reported across northwest Iowa with the Department of Transportation advising no travel for several hours in the afternoon. The high winds also produced some spotty power outages. Numerous schools delayed release to allow for conditions to improve.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of a quarter mile or less was common along Interstate 90 across Jackson County as northwest winds reached 35 to 50 mph and interacted with periods of light snow during the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved from Kansas northeastward to the Great Lakes on December 28 into the morning hours of December 29. The system brought a leading and more prominent band of snowfall during the morning and midday on December 28, followed by a break in precipitation. As the system deepened during the evening, the band of snowfall wrapped back southward through far eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. When the current and previous snow combined with winds gusting from 40 to 60 mph, widespread blizzard conditions developed from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa. State Departments of Transportation advised no travel across southern Minnesota and portions of Iowa from late morning on the 28th through mid-morning of the 29th. Numerous roadways were closed due to numerous crashes. Total snowfall reached as high as 2 to 5 inches in much of southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, with 1 to 2 inches along the Missouri River and Interstate 29 corridors and lesser amounts toward the mid James River valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two episodes of extended blizzard conditions occurred over a slightly less than 24 hour period, with a slight improvement in conditions during the late afternoon to early evening. Gusty northwest winds from 35 to 50 mph were concurrent with light to moderate snowfall, which totaled 3.0 inches at Windom. With frequent whiteout conditions reported, travel was not advised.
