EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system would provide rainfall in the early morning hours that would transition to snow behind an arctic front producing strong wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph. Given the wet road surfaces and rapidly falling temperatures, flash freezing concerns existed for areas across central Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro. Across portions of south-central and southern Minnesota, strong winds caused falling snow to turn into blowing snow leading to blizzard conditions. ||The strongest wind gusts from this event were: |Willmar at 60 mph (Kandiyohi)|Redwood Falls at 55 mph (Redwood)|Montevideo at 51 mph (Chippewa)|Olivia at 49 mph (Renville)|New Ulm at 49 mph (Brown)|Le Sueur at 45 mph (Le Sueur).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow combined with strong wind gusts caused blowing snow and reduced visibility to below one-quarter mile for several hours. The AWOS station located at the Mankato Regional Airport recorded several hours of falling snow with wind gusts ranging between 35 to near 50 mph that afternoon. The strongest gust recorded at the station was 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface cold front moved across the northern plains on December 12th. The front featured a brief period of light snow in the early morning across western Minnesota, then a mixture of light snow and freezing precipitation across eastern Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro around the morning rush-hour. The greatest impacts occurred across western Minnesota where a cold airmass increased winds to 25-35 mph. In areas where snow had just recently fallen, the increased winds created periods of reduced visibility as a result of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS located at the Morris Municipal Airport reported gusty winds of 36 mph and visibility below one-half mile due to blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small band of primarily freezing rain and sleet developed across southwestern Minnesota then tracked east across central and eastern Minnesota. The band of precipitation produced an hour or two of a light wintry mix resulting in minor ice and sleet accumulation before changing into all snow. As a result of the freezing rain, slick travel conditions existed during the morning commute. Travel advisories were issued by MnDOT for portions of south-central MN due to slippery roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation on their snowboard in Woodbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system would provide rainfall in the early morning hours that would transition to snow behind an arctic front producing strong wind gusts of 40 to 60 mph. Given the wet road surfaces and rapidly falling temperatures, flash freezing concerns existed for areas across central Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro. Across portions of south-central and southern Minnesota, strong winds caused falling snow to turn into blowing snow leading to blizzard conditions. ||The strongest wind gusts from this event were: |Willmar at 60 mph (Kandiyohi)|Redwood Falls at 55 mph (Redwood)|Montevideo at 51 mph (Chippewa)|Olivia at 49 mph (Renville)|New Ulm at 49 mph (Brown)|Le Sueur at 45 mph (Le Sueur).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow combined with strong 40 to 50 mph wind gusts caused blowing snow and whiteout conditions for multiple hours. The nearest AWOS station located at Fairmont Municipal Airport recorded a 49 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lake-effect snow band developed off the Twin Ports over western Lake Superior and moved on-shore during the afternoon and evening hours of the 29th. The band stalled out over the area instead of moving to the east as forecasted and led to snowfall accumulations of 4 to 6 inches over western portions of Duluth and into the Proctor area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy band of lake effect snow set up over Duluth and into the Proctor area the afternoon and evening hours of the 29th. This band was expected to quickly shift to the east, but ended up stalling out over the same area for an extended period of time. This led to snowfall totals of 4 to 6 inches in these areas with a high report of 6.5 inches in the Morgan Park neighborhood of Duluth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure produced a late fall system that brought a round of rain and heavy snow, as well as very gusty winds to the Upper Midwest. The combination of heavy falling snow and northwest winds gusting to around 40 to 50 mph resulted in significant travel impacts, especially across western and central Minnesota where the strongest winds overlapped with falling snow.||The greatest snowfall amounts generally were reported along and north of the I-94 corridor, topping out around 5 to 9 inches. ||The top snowfall reports were:|9.5 inches near Camp Ripley, MN (Morrison).|8.1 inches in Bertha, MN (Todd).|8.0 inches southwest of Vineland, MN (Morrison).|7.2 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|7.1 inches near Bock, MN (Mille Lacs).|7.0 inches in Carlos, MN (Douglas).|7.0 inches in Little Falls, MN (Morrison).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy snow and strong winds impacted the county Tuesday afternoon into Wednesday morning. Snowfall amounts around 4 to 6 inches were reported across the county, and wind gusts around 35 to 45 mph were measured throughout the event at Alexandria Regional Airport. The Minnesota Department of Transportation advised against non-essential travel Friday evening as road conditions deteriorated quickly due to periods of brief whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed across west central Minnesota around mid-day. Both the Big Stone and Traverse County Sheriffs issued a No Travel Advised statement, which continued through 6:00 am Wednesday. The Wheaton AWOS and the Minnesota Department of Transportation Road Weather Information System (RWIS) station 3 miles west-southwest of Graceville both recorded visibility dropping to between 1/4 and 1/2 mile, with peak wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph. Traverse County also saw a total of 547 customers lose power during the storm. A total of 8.5 inches of snow was recorded by the COOP observer 6 miles northwest of Wheaton, while the Artichoke Lake COOP observer recorded 6.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Stone County Sheriff reported white-out conditions and issued a No Travel Advised statement for Big Stone County. The Sheriff also reported several vehicles in the ditch and a semi jackknifed across Highway 12 east of Ortonville. The Minnesota Department of Transportation Road Weather Information System (RWIS) 3 miles west-southwest of Graceville recorded visibility dropping to between 1/4 and 1/2 mile with wind gusts as high as 39 mph starting around 1:00 pm, before the sensor lost power at 2:10 pm. The COOP observer at Artichoke Lake also reported that blizzard conditions were ongoing at 6:00 pm with a total accumulation of 6.2 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index readings reached an afternoon peak on July 27 as high as 107 degrees near Mountain Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. Combined with the strong winds, visibility briefly dropped to one-half mile or less in numerous locations north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 43 mph at times at a Minnesota Road Weather Information System site 1 mile west of Lake Benton.

This event occurred on the Tuesday before thanksgiving causing more of an impacted with the increased amount of travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure produced a late fall system that brought a round of rain and heavy snow, as well as very gusty winds to the Upper Midwest. The combination of heavy falling snow and northwest winds gusting to around 40 to 50 mph resulted in significant travel impacts, especially across western and central Minnesota where the strongest winds overlapped with falling snow.||The greatest snowfall amounts generally were reported along and north of the I-94 corridor, topping out around 5 to 9 inches. ||The top snowfall reports were:|9.5 inches near Camp Ripley, MN (Morrison).|8.1 inches in Bertha, MN (Todd).|8.0 inches southwest of Vineland, MN (Morrison).|7.2 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|7.1 inches near Bock, MN (Mille Lacs).|7.0 inches in Carlos, MN (Douglas).|7.0 inches in Little Falls, MN (Morrison).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy snow and strong winds impacted the county Tuesday afternoon into Wednesday morning. Snowfall amounts around 1 to 4 inches were estimated across the county, and wind gusts around 35 to 50 mph were measured throughout the event at Benson Municipal Airport. The Minnesota Department of Transportation advised against non-essential travel Friday evening as road conditions deteriorated quickly due to periods of brief whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration late fall snowstorm developed across southern Minnesota late Friday night and spread northeast over the course of Saturday morning. The snow fell for around 24 to 32 hours, staying mostly light to moderate rates. The greatest snowfall amounts were reported across southern Minnesota, topping out around 8 to 12 inches. The Twin Cities metro generally received anywhere from 3��� in the northern suburbs to 6��� in the southern suburbs, which was topped off by a last minute burst of heavy snow for a few hours Sunday morning.||Breezy winds at the end of the event meant blowing and drifting snow made it hard to get accurate measurements. The main impact from this storm was difficult travel for the weekend following Thanksgiving.||The top snowfall reports were:|12.0 inches in Blue Earth, MN (Faribault).|10.5 inches in Fairmont, MN (Martin).|9.8 inches in Winnebago, MN (Faribault).|8.4 inches in Ellendale, MN (Steele).|8.0 inches in Northfield, MN (Rice).|7.5 inches in New Prague, MN (Scott).|7.5 inches near Kasota, MN (Le Sueur).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent light snow lasted for over 24 hours, with strong winds gusting to 35 mph near the end of the event. Snowfall totals across the county were estimated around 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. Combined with the strong winds, visibility briefly dropped to one-half mile or less in numerous locations north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached around 40 mph at times at an Automated Weather Observing System site at Windom, and Minnesota Road Weather Information System sites near Mountain Lake and Jeffers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture pooled in the vicinity of a nearly stationary boundary early on July 21, which slowly worked northward as a warm front over the next two days while a large upper level ridge expanded across the Northern Plains. Most areas experienced heat index values from 100 to 115 degrees, with the most consistent and highest heat index values on the afternoon of July 23 across far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous heat index readings reached afternoon peaks as high as 111 degrees 6 miles south of Westbrook on July 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northeastern Minnesota the morning of the 23rd ahead of a cold front. Storms began in Cass County where they produced tree damage and a measured gust of 64 mph in Walker. As they moved to the northeast, they lost their strength, but became heavy rain producers which led to some minor flooding in the city of Ely during the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert on Trout Lake Road (Forest Road 308) between Marsh Lake and Trout Lake was washed out leading to the closure of Forest Road 308 until repairs were able to be made.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions across parts of the Northland from Tuesday November 26 through Thanksgiving, Thursday November 27. The storm began as a mix of rain and snow during the day Tuesday, transitioning to all snow Tuesday evening. Snowfall rates of over one inch per hour occured in areas from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Twin Ports/I-35 corridor and into northwest Wisconsin, with strong winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph causing blowing snow to reduce visibility and leading to drifting snow even well after the snow ended in northeast Minnesota on Wednesday morning. Lake-effect snow continued in northwest Wisconsin through Wednesday and lingering into Thursday morning, with blizzard conditions at times on Wednesday across parts of northwest Wisconsin - especially eastern Douglas County and the higher elevation terrain of Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While the bulk of the snowfall fell to areas south and east of northern St. Louis County, some areas did see some higher amounts. Upwards of 7 inches of accumulation was reported in Ely with this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure developed across northern South Dakota and pushed eastward into Minnesota on the night of November 25-26. A very intense pressure gradient on the western flank of the system produced widespread winds which gusted from 50 to 75 mph, with the highest wind gusts noted through the Missouri River corridor and along the Buffalo Ridge. Light snowfall wrapped through areas of east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening, with amounts varying from a dusting along Interstate 90 to 1 to 2 inches near U.S. Highway 14. Combined with the strong winds, visibility briefly dropped to one-half mile or less in numerous locations north of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds from 40 to 60 mph and a period of evening light snowfall led to several hours with visibility reduced to one mile or less, briefly around one-half mile near Trosky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions across parts of the Northland from Tuesday November 26 through Thanksgiving, Thursday November 27. The storm began as a mix of rain and snow during the day Tuesday, transitioning to all snow Tuesday evening. Snowfall rates of over one inch per hour occured in areas from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Twin Ports/I-35 corridor and into northwest Wisconsin, with strong winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph causing blowing snow to reduce visibility and leading to drifting snow even well after the snow ended in northeast Minnesota on Wednesday morning. Lake-effect snow continued in northwest Wisconsin through Wednesday and lingering into Thursday morning, with blizzard conditions at times on Wednesday across parts of northwest Wisconsin - especially eastern Douglas County and the higher elevation terrain of Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals around southern Lake County were in the 4 to 7 inch range with the snow being heavy and wet. A high report of 7.5 inches was recorded about 8 miles northwest of Two Harbors. A man clearing snow was killed when a tree laden with the heavy wet snow snapped in the strong winds and landed on him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first snow of the season occurred November 8 as a clipper system interacted with a polar cold-air intrusion across the Northern Plains. Light snow first developed during the early morning hours and continued into the early afternoon. A broad swath of snow from 3-5 inches fell from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer. Relatively warm ground temperatures limited accumulations on area roadways with most major arteries clear by the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant and prolonged winter storm moved into the region late on November 28th, bringing moderate snow for a roughly 36 hour period before exiting the region by the early morning hours on November 30th. This storm was particularly impactful as it occurred right after Thanksgiving resulting in extra difficulties for holiday travel. Overall, the storm brought snowfall accumulations of 5 to 10 inches across much of southeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This significant winter storm brought between 6 and 9 inches of snow to Olmsted County between November 28th and November 30th. The highest snowfall report in the county was 8.9 inches 3 miles west-southwest of Chester. Additionally, two vehicle accidents occurred in the county during the storm that resulted in a total of 2 non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November saw the return of D2 Severe Drought conditions across parts of northeastern Minnesota following an extended period of below normal precipitation extending back to the late summer. Starting with the update on the 4th, D2 Severe Drought was introduced across much of Carlton County and portions of southern St. Louis and Pine counties. With the update on the 18th, D2 Severe Drought conditions expanded into portions of Aitkin County as well. For the month, precipitation was around a quarter to three-quarters of an inch below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions were introduced across almost all of Carlton County and into the Duluth area of southeastern southern St. Louis County with the update on the 4th. With the update on the 18th, D2 Severe Drought further expanded encompassing all of Carlton County and roughly the southern half of southern St. Louis County. No additional changes in drought category were observed over the remainder of the month. Precipitation for the month was around 0.25 to 0.75 inches below normal in this area with the dry pattern extending back into the summer months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure produced a late fall system that brought a round of rain and heavy snow, as well as very gusty winds to the Upper Midwest. The combination of heavy falling snow and northwest winds gusting to around 40 to 50 mph resulted in significant travel impacts, especially across western and central Minnesota where the strongest winds overlapped with falling snow.||The greatest snowfall amounts generally were reported along and north of the I-94 corridor, topping out around 5 to 9 inches. ||The top snowfall reports were:|9.5 inches near Camp Ripley, MN (Morrison).|8.1 inches in Bertha, MN (Todd).|8.0 inches southwest of Vineland, MN (Morrison).|7.2 inches in Long Prairie, MN (Todd).|7.1 inches near Bock, MN (Mille Lacs).|7.0 inches in Carlos, MN (Douglas).|7.0 inches in Little Falls, MN (Morrison).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow and strong winds impacted the county Tuesday evening into Wednesday morning. Snowfall amounts around 1 to 3 inches were estimated across the county, and wind gusts around 30 to 40 mph were measured throughout the event at nearby Glencoe Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant and prolonged winter storm moved into the region late on November 28th, bringing moderate snow for a roughly 36 hour period before exiting the region by the early morning hours on November 30th. This storm was particularly impactful as it occurred right after Thanksgiving resulting in extra difficulties for holiday travel. Overall, the storm brought snowfall accumulations of 5 to 10 inches across much of southeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This significant winter storm brought between 6 and 8 inches of snow to Dodge County between November 28th and November 30th. The highest report in the county was in 4 miles northwest of Rock Dell where 7.7 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions across parts of the Northland from Tuesday November 26 through Thanksgiving, Thursday November 27. The storm began as a mix of rain and snow during the day Tuesday, transitioning to all snow Tuesday evening. Snowfall rates of over one inch per hour occured in areas from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Twin Ports/I-35 corridor and into northwest Wisconsin, with strong winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph causing blowing snow to reduce visibility and leading to drifting snow even well after the snow ended in northeast Minnesota on Wednesday morning. Lake-effect snow continued in northwest Wisconsin through Wednesday and lingering into Thursday morning, with blizzard conditions at times on Wednesday across parts of northwest Wisconsin - especially eastern Douglas County and the higher elevation terrain of Bayfield, Ashland, and Iron counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals for the event were in the 4 to 8 inch range across much of Pine County with the highest totals across the northern part of the county. A high report of 10.2 inches was received from near Kerrick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening on the Independence Day Federal Holiday resulted in damaging wind gusts across northwest and west central Minnesota. Flash flooding also caused widespread impacts to Bemidji, MN as a result of heavy rain and tree damage debris from the June 20-21 derecho blocking streets and storm drains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sentinel-2 satellite imagery reveals high probability of tornado damage at this location within southeastern portions of Minnesota's Beltrami Island State Forest. Damage occurred in very rural and undeveloped area, with no reports of any damage to structures received. Damage indicators unknown at this time despite obvious damage to trees via satellite imagery. Time estimated via KMVX radar. EM confirmed road was blocked by tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed across west central Minnesota around mid-day. Both the Big Stone and Traverse County Sheriffs issued a No Travel Advised statement, which continued through 6:00 am Wednesday. The Wheaton AWOS and the Minnesota Department of Transportation Road Weather Information System (RWIS) station 3 miles west-southwest of Graceville both recorded visibility dropping to between 1/4 and 1/2 mile, with peak wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph. Traverse County also saw a total of 547 customers lose power during the storm. A total of 8.5 inches of snow was recorded by the COOP observer 6 miles northwest of Wheaton, while the Artichoke Lake COOP observer recorded 6.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wheaton AWOS recorded visibility of 1/4 to 1/2 mile from 12:30 pm through 5:30 pm with a peak wind gust of 35 mph at 4:33 pm. The Traverse County Sheriff issued a No Travel Advised statement at 2:30 pm which lasted through 6:00 am Wednesday due to a combination of high winds, low visibility, and slick road conditions. Traverse County also experienced a loss of power, with an outage total of 547 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moved across the southern half of Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro during the late afternoon and evening hours of September 21st. The storm environment consisted of favorable instability, low cloud bases, and some veering winds with height. Although the shear profile was considered only marginal (weak) at the time, a few storms did manage to become severe. Two swaths of large hail ranging from quarter (1-inch) to Hen Egg (2-inch) sized hail fell across portions of the southern Twin Cities metro and in Steele in Waseca Counties.||Two-inch diameter hailstones were reported on the east side of Lake Minnetonka (Hennepin), one mile south of Woodbury (Washington), and six miles NW of New Richland (Waseca).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of hail covering the ground, with the largest stone being quarter-sized. Also tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms produced several cases of large hail and funnel clouds, as well as two tornadoes in Nicollet county during the afternoon and evening on Monday, June 16th. The largest reported hail (3.25 inches) occurred with a storm the developed over McLeod county and quickly strengthened over Carver county. There was also significant wind damage in the St Cloud area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 12 residents in the city of Alden reported water in their basements. The City of Alden used pumps to bypass the sewer system to alleviate overload to the sewage ponds. This flooding was a result of heavy rain that occurred throughout the day on June 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 115 degrees with a maximum heat index of 115 degrees at personal weather stations 6 miles northwest of Orleans and in Lakefield on June 21, and 107 degrees at Round Lake on June 21.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 109 degrees with a maximum heat index of 109 degrees at a personal weather station near Ivanhoe on June 21 and 103 degrees at a Road Weather Information System site near Hendricks on June 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed near the South Dakota and Minnesota border during the early evening on June 28th, then quickly strengthened into discrete supercells as they progressed into west central Minnesota. These storms produced scattered reports of large hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and three tornadoes. ||As the evening progressed, the supercells merged into clusters and eventually into long line that produced more widespread wind damage and five tornadoes in Carver County. The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree near 1st street NE and Calder Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 112 degrees with a maximum heat index of 112 degrees at a personal weather station near Westbrook on June 20, 111 degrees at a personal weather station near Storden on June 21, 107 degrees near Jeffers and 106 degrees near Mountain Lake at Road Weather Information System sites on June 21, and 106 degrees at the Automated Weather Observing System site near Windom on June 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed near the South Dakota and Minnesota border during the early evening on June 28th, then quickly strengthened into discrete supercells as they progressed into west central Minnesota. These storms produced scattered reports of large hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and three tornadoes. ||As the evening progressed, the supercells merged into clusters and eventually into long line that produced more widespread wind damage and five tornadoes in Carver County. The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branch snapped, about 6 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moved across the southern half of Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro during the late afternoon and evening hours of September 21st. The storm environment consisted of favorable instability, low cloud bases, and some veering winds with height. Although the shear profile was considered only marginal (weak) at the time, a few storms did manage to become severe. Two swaths of large hail ranging from quarter (1-inch) to Hen Egg (2-inch) sized hail fell across portions of the southern Twin Cities metro and in Steele in Waseca Counties.||Two-inch diameter hailstones were reported on the east side of Lake Minnetonka (Hennepin), one mile south of Woodbury (Washington), and six miles NW of New Richland (Waseca).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a large amount of hail, the largest around quarter sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed late afternoon along a slow-moving frontal boundary. Even with fairly weak deep-layer shear, strong instability resulted in large hail. Drier air and advancing cold pools later in development produced spotty damaging winds. High moisture content and slowly progressing storms also produced some locally heavy rainfall with rates briefly from 3 to 5 inches per hour.

Scattered thunderstorms developed across southern and central Minnesota during the early evening on Thursday, June 12. There was a report of hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter in Watonwan County, but the most notable storm formed over northeastern McLeod County and produced an 86 mph gust at Hutchinson Airport. The isolated winds were likely due to a downburst which did down several trees and powerlines in Hutchinson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of September 4, a broken line of thunderstorms developed and produced gusty winds across western Minnesota. The strongest gust was 62 mph at the Granite Falls airport. ||As the storms moved east, the produced two weak tornadoes. These tornadoes caused mainly crop damage, but there was also some damage to a couple groves of trees, and some roof damage to a hog barn. The tornadoes were both rated EF0, with peak winds of 70 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Satellite and drone imagery confirmed a brief tornado in a cornfield southeast of Lafayette MN. It traveled east for 3.5 miles and damaged the roof of a hog barn and produced crop damage along its path. The tornado was rated EF0.

The tornadoes were both rated EF0, with peak winds of 70 to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in a field and hit a farmstead, knocking down and breaking multiple trees across the property. The time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed on the afternoon of June 30 over northern Minnesota and Wisconsin, traveling southeast through the evening. One of the stronger storms moved through the western and southern Twin Cities metro, knocking down a cottonwood tree just southeast of Prior Lake. It was determined that these wind gusts were sub-severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter reported a cottonwood tree down over a road in Cleary Lake Regional Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale mid-to-upper-level trough was moving across the northern plains on the afternoon of June 26th. Immediately ahead of this progressing wave was a mesoscale convective vortex moving out of the Missouri River Valley. Ahead of this MCV was a warm front that supplied sufficient shear and low-level moisture across southern Minnesota into northern Iowa. A low topped supercell developed within a line of thunderstorms stretched along the I-35 corridor.||An EF-0 tornado developed just northwest of Twin Lakes and traveled over five miles and was approximately 75 yds wide. Maximum winds were estimated to be 80 mph, damaging two farm sheds before destroying farm outbuildings and overturning a trailer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser took video of a tornado near its beginning, just northwest of Twin Lakes. It moved across two farms, tree groves, and fields. At the farms, two sheds were damaged from falling trees. The tornado entered the southeast edge of Albert Lea, taking down more trees and destroying a chicken coop. A trailer was knocked over before the tornado dissipated. Drone video of the damage in the southeast side of Albert Lea was reviewed. The maximum wind speed was estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into southern Minnesota on the afternoon of Wednesday, June 25th. Strong surface heating to the south of the front with dew points in the 70s allowed for ample instability to develop across the area.  Scattered thunderstorms developed in response to the instability and flash flooding occurred in western and east central MN. Strong low-level shear allowed these low-topped supercells to rotate and produce four EF-0s and one EF-1 tornadoes across Faribault and Freeborn Counties in southern Minnesota. ||Most tornadoes were on the ground between one to three miles and approximately 25 yds wide. The longest tornado path was an EF-1 that occurred west-southwest of Hartland in Freeborn County for about 5.2 miles with peak winds at 105 mph. The tornado damaged crops, power poles, and several farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was caught on video in Faribault County, just north of Interstate 90. It moved across fields before moving into Freeborn County, where it hit some trees. Satellite imagery confirmed the tornado path. Based on the tree damage, the maximum wind speed was estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level wave had moved into western Minnesota and increased moisture and surface-based instability during the afternoon of September 1st. This led to diurnal convective activity mainly west of the I-35 corridor for that afternoon. The threat of severe weather was low, however weak shear and steep lapse rates existed in the 0-3km level, which led to a brief funnel cloud developing with one cell approximately two miles east of Blomkest, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of funnel cloud approximately two miles east of Blomkest submitted via Facebook messenger.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in response to the instability and flash flooding occurred in western and east central MN. Strong low-level shear allowed these low-topped supercells to rotate and produce four EF-0s and one EF-1 tornadoes across Faribault and Freeborn Counties in southern Minnesota. ||Most tornadoes were on the ground between one to three miles and approximately 25 yds wide. The longest tornado path was an EF-1 that occurred west-southwest of Hartland in Freeborn County for about 5.2 miles with peak winds at 105 mph. The tornado damaged crops, power poles, and several farm outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and some members of the public took photos of a tornado that crossed the southern portion of Freeborn Lake. Before crossing the lake, it damaged trees at an abandoned farmstead. After crossing the lake, it damaged some corn on the eastern side of the lake before it dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms moved across the southern half of Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro during the late afternoon and evening hours of September 21st. The storm environment consisted of favorable instability, low cloud bases, and some veering winds with height. Although the shear profile was considered only marginal (weak) at the time, a few storms did manage to become severe. Two swaths of large hail ranging from quarter (1-inch) to Hen Egg (2-inch) sized hail fell across portions of the southern Twin Cities metro and in Steele in Waseca Counties.||Two-inch diameter hailstones were reported on the east side of Lake Minnetonka (Hennepin), one mile south of Woodbury (Washington), and six miles NW of New Richland (Waseca).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chasers and trained spotters (including fire and law personnel) took photos and video of a tornado that began west-southwest of Hartland. It moved across one farm where large branches were knocked down, then moved just northwest of Hartland, barely missing the city and two more farms. It eventually hit one farm at 42.8204, -93.4657 before turning a little more northward and dissipating north of 325th St. At the farm, several outbuildings were destroyed. Just northeast of the farm, power poles were knocked over. The path was confirmed through drone video and satellite imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed north of the Iron Range into the Minnesota Arrowhead during the afternoon of the 8th. Aside from some small hail and a lightning strike damaging some fiber optic lines, the storms remained below severe limits.

Thunderstorms developed north of the Iron Range into the Minnesota Arrowhead during the afternoon of the 8th. Aside from some small hail and a lightning strike damaging some fiber optic lines, the storms remained below severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike near the Gunflint Trail and Soderberg Lane damaged about 2,000 feet of underground fiber optic cable causing a loss of phone and internet service to customers from that point to the end of the Gunflint Trail. Service was restored after about 14 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm to the north of a warm front moved across the Brainerd Lakes area during the morning of the 12th. The storm produced quite a bit of hail, with the largest to the size of ping pong balls. In some locations, there was so much hail covering the ground it looked like snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was accumulating and looked like snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge rider pattern continued across the region, with a volatile but slightly capped environment in place heading into the evening of June 28. Remnant boundaries from convection the prior morning provided focus for the new storm development, which had up to 3000 J/kg MUCAPE and around 30 knots of effective shear. Storms remained fairly restrained in the Missouri River Valley fighting the warmest air aloft and weakest shear profiles. Storms coming from northeast South Dakota became the most organized, with a small bowing segment producing damaging winds up to around 65 mph. Other late night storms strengthened moving from far southeast South Dakota across northwest Iowa with wind gusts up to 80 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind gusts broke off several 2 inch diameter tree limbs.

A weak mid-level wave dropped slowly southeast across northern Minnesota on the afternoon and evening of September 21 and into an environment with 35 kts of deep-layer shear and 1000-1500 J/kg MUCAPE. Scattered slow-moving thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of a diffuse warm frontal boundary and produced hail up to 1.75 to 2 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed near the South Dakota and Minnesota border during the early evening on June 28th, then quickly strengthened into discrete supercells as they progressed into west central Minnesota. These storms produced scattered reports of large hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and three tornadoes. ||As the evening progressed, the supercells merged into clusters and eventually into long line that produced more widespread wind damage and five tornadoes in Carver County. The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed over a field and moved toward a home where it knocked down a couple trees and produced some other tree damage. Four different videos from various locations enabled determination of its track to the east, as it moved over more fields before dissipating. The maximum estimated wind was 90 mph.

The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed just west of a farm and tracked east-southeast, before turning to the southeast and dissipating. At the farm, it damaged outbuildings, taking portions of the roofs and walls. It mangled trees, knocked over three irrigation pivots, and ripped through three turkey barns. Video was provided of the tornado. Photos and drone video of the damage were provided to determine the specific track. The maximum estimated wind was 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse lifted northward through the Central Plains within a high amplitude upper air pattern and around 30 knots of mainly deep-layer speed shear. Afternoon surface heating resulted in 1000-1500 J/kg MLCAPE and almost an equal amount of DCAPE given broad mid-level dry air. The thunderstorms that developed were relatively brief, but produced sporadic damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured as high as 70 mph at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Beaver Creek.

These storms produced scattered reports of large hail, thunderstorm wind damage, and three tornadoes. ||As the evening progressed, the supercells merged into clusters and eventually into long line that produced more widespread wind damage and five tornadoes in Carver County. The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado broke or uprooted dozens of trees. It hit one farm, damaging several outbuildings. Roof panels were removed, the door was blown off a large shed, and other damage was noted per drone imagery. The drone imagery also helped indicate the location of some of the tree damage. An old barn also collapsed. The start of the tornado was estimated based on a distant view from drone video. The maximum estimated wind speed was 85 mph.

The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Touchdown was likely in a field northwest of Goose Lake. The most substantial tree damage occurred along the northwest shore of Lake Waconia. The tornado then tracked across County Rd 92 south of Crown College before ending somewhere between Parley Lake Rd and the western edge of Carver Park Reserve. The maximum estimated wind speed was 95 mph.

The storms gradually weakened after moving into Wisconsin and south central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touchdown likely occurred in the northern part of Carver Park Reserve before tracking across portions of Stone Lake and Lake Zumbra. Tree damage occurred along the shores of Lake Zumbra & residences on Zumbra Drive. The most extensive tree damage occurred at Rolling Acres Rd and Highway 7 where numerous trees were uprooted or were broken. The tornado terminated over Lake Minnewashta. The maximum estimated winds were 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With less than 10 knots of deep-layer shear, isolated thunderstorms were very slow moving along existing outflow boundaries and resulted in several complimentary boundary interactions. One such interaction resulted in a stronger and more persistent updraft, with hail up to nickel size across southwest Minnesota.

The upper-level pattern experienced amplification of an eastern U.S. ridge and western U.S. trough, which locked a low-level frontal boundary in place from western Nebraska through central South Dakota into the Great Lakes. This allowed for heat to spread northward as very high dew point readings pooled across the area with the recent wet conditions. This multi-day dangerous heat event featured daytime heat index readings from 100 to 120 degrees, with nighttime low temperatures remaining from the mid 70s to lower 80s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 105 to 108 degrees with a maximum heat index of 108 degrees at a Road Weather Information System site near Rushmore on June 21 and 104 degrees at an Automated Weather Observing System site at Worthington on June 20.

Multiple rounds of storms produced several cases of large hail and funnel clouds, as well as two tornadoes in Nicollet county during the afternoon and evening on Monday, June 16th. The largest reported hail (3.25 inches) occurred with a storm the developed over McLeod county and quickly strengthened over Carver county. There was also significant wind damage in the St Cloud area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms developed on the nose of a low-level jet, with 1500-2000 J/kg elevated instability and deep-layer shear in excess of 50 kts. A brief period of marginally large hail and severe wind gusts occurred with the storm cluster from northwest of Brookings east-southeast to near Lake Benton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a few trees in Hendricks.
